text: At the end of a long line of divine tyrants and feudal lords.
Feels like being at the end of history itself, taking a dive off into a chasm of hope.
I have no opinions on it.
Yeah, yeah. Say that as much as you want, you're still living in a Freestrider world.
Obviously. You've already been obsessed with it for years at this point.
You may repeat that as much as you want, but the Freestrider slogans carved into the back of your skull prove otherwise. Like, purely bodily speaking.
A bunch of stuff. Personal freedom. The right to climb the ladder.
Free trade is a part of it. Letting people, and the companies they own, spread their economic roots without big government stomping on them.
Then you've got democracy, which is like, the most liberal thing out of them all.
Mainly because it allows people to actually pick and choose their leadership, instead of it being determined by gods, spells, and steel. As is the historical precedent.
Not that your thoughts have any real impact on the city.
Unless you get back to writing, of course.
So we're turning Freestrider no matter what?
True. Their power is just too overwhelming.
Writing? Why would I ever perform such a degenerate act?
And where does that power come from?
Exactly. They're on the right side of history!
Okay, I think you need to rethink your whole mindset here.
Look. You've got some bruises on your fingers from last time. But really, that's just proof that you were on the right trail!
Freestriderism is the future. That much is clear.
What you saw happened in that fancy room, between Viira and her glowing guest?
That's going to be the main conflict going forward. Not some scuffle with Azgal youth students, or rigid theocratic bureaucrats grasping to hold on to their mansions...
No, the future obstacles are out there.
As Freestriderism creeps across the Coast, there will be a lot of powers ready to meet it. Both head-on, but also in more sinister ways...
As shown, by the Golden Horde. They're clearly trying to co-opt the whole thing.
I liked Ambassador Ivcx. Seemed like a nice fellow.
The Golden Horde is a scourge upon the Coast. They should not be affiliated with Freestriderism at all.
We all need to make concessions to get our policies enacted.
Viira is doing the best she can.
Your taste in people is questionable.
Look, in an ideal world, yeah. That'd be swell.
It's all about being smart. Pick your battles. Like the purple one says: be smooth, or whatever.
Oh, sure. She's clearly struggling though.
Reeds is in a precarious position, believe it or not. It'll be all too easy for the liberal, wondrous dream to collapse in on itself.
With enemies on all sides, within and without, you need to band together with your fellow Freestriders and FIGHT BACK.
Or, if that doesn't work, grab what you can while the grabbin's good. Then jump out the window in a blaze of glory.
Wait, so... am I doing this for me, or for the liberal ideal?
Hells yeah. (Feel ready to grab, and grab again.)
That does sound like it could collapse at any second.
One and the same, buddy. One and the same.
Just like in the good old days!
And somehow, over these past fifty-something years of crazy, radical Freestriderism, that just hasn't happened.
You remember how Freestriderism was born, right?
Let's go over it again. (Listen to the political exposition.)
It all goes back to the Arcanists. As everything tends to do.

Most of the their states were essentially built upon aristocratic structures. Feudal states, turned magocratic. Wizards need esoteric educations, right? Who can afford such an education? A nobleman, that's who.
It's all economics. All the way to the top.
All the biggest Arcanist houses were all former nobles after all.
Of course not. But it can only be cultivated via learning.

So they spent the early Arcane Era building up their power. House Prax. House Hagginshags. House Diminorii. All led by men descending from long lines of royals. We've even got a theocracy, with The Shadow Lord, turning into another magocracy in the end.
But with all that magical might, despite reaching the pinnacle of esoteric knowledge towards the second century of their era...
Don't get me wrong. Magic is real neat. Cutting mountains in half, flipping them over, building cities on them? Awesome. Infinite food? Convenient!
But you can only get so far with a top-down structure.
Imagine you've got twenty people working under you. You assign them tasks- to, I don't know, transport your magically created bread to a thousand hungry mouths around the city.
You, alone, could probably organize that. Twenty people ain't that much.
But what if it wasn't twenty people feeding a thousand people- what if it was a five thousand people, making sure a hundred thousand people got fed their daily piece of fake bread?
I'd still be able to do that.
Yeah, yeah, brag to someone outside your own damn head.
The average Arcane Era city had a hell of a lot more.
That's two words. And yeah, you're right.

The point is: even the smartest, most glorious super-genius wizard-god-king will eventually be spread thin. With the crazy scale of modern society - and especially with infinite food you can bet your ass that people start breeding like giant rabbits - the Arcanists just couldn't keep up.
Divine Era kingdoms never reached those levels, even with walking gods at the helms. So the Arcanists did what they had to.
The old aristocrats still held most of the power, but they got dragged kicking and screaming into a world where anyone could become a wizard.
And more importantly, those nobodies could also become stinking rich.
That's how you get the necropolis manufacturies. The ocean trade network. Basically the entire built-in-a-factory way of life we enjoy today.
Did the Industrial Revolution begin in Old Diminor?
But wouldn't that just lead to magocracy, led by those with money?
Infinite food sounds lovely. We should go back to that.
Go on. (Continue listening to the story of Freestriderism.)
I think I've heard enough. (Skip it.)
Nah. Technically, there's been mechanical industries on Jor since the Gate War. That's how the orcs managed to join in against eldritch aberrations and divine beings.
But our modern revolution sprouted when those same orcs rediscovered their roots.
Bloodcity Bay used to be a scattered collection of states, but in the Divine Era the warlords turned it into the thing we know today.
Which includes a shit-ton of industry.
Sure, but as long as money flows towards merit, then you'll have a fair game going.
You'd think that. But here's the deal: the second we entered into somewhat modernized agriculture, food scarcity stopped being a production issue.
It became, and remains, entirely an issue of logistics. We've got so much damn food being made, and the only problem is getting it into the mouths of the people.

Magical food then becomes like rotten waterfall: if bread is free, then the people growing the wheat can't get paid as much. Thus eventually they'll stop growing it. Who gets most impacted when food production disappears from an economy? The working class. On top of that, if the food supply becomes entirely centralized with spellcasters, then food production gets inverted from the natural method - instead of food being created rurally and moved into urban areas, instead food has to be moved from cities to smaller communities.

And once again, you'll also get the problem of top-down governing. By centralizing food production into a handful of extremely powerful wizards (or maybe a few thousand, at the peak of certain city-states) then even the most minor mistake can cause widespread famines.
Which is why every single city-state that attempted this 'free food' method failed.
And any that try it in the future will fail too.
Large enough systems require decentralization and economic liberalism to even function. At least in the long term.

Liberalism is thus inevitable in a modern world. Even the most illiberal states today use parts of Freestrider theorem to rule. Even esoteric autocracies like the Golden Horde or oligarchies like Bloodcity Bay.
You'll bet your ass they're all hopping on the free-trade wagon. Even if they're desperate to hold onto their outdated regimes.

This was a period when national borders didn't really exist yet. It was more of... pseudo feudal thing going on. Royalty owning land, and the serfs on it. Not so much 'folk belonging to nationstates' as we see it today.

But, those early magocrats still wanted centralized control, so they pushed to drive everyone into their cities. In that century of industrialization, literally millions of humans moved from scattered rural towns into one of the capitals of Old Diminor.
Hells, they had humans from all around the Coast moving - we're talking entire civilization uprooted - just to go live in the modern world of the Arcanists.
To work in factories. To have cheap glowstones and running water. To live in peace, yes, but also to have actual personal freedoms.

People could move between city-states, for the most part. A man could rise above his station and make something out of himself- even if the top gigs were all held by those in power. There's a reason we don't call it mago-feudalism, or whatever. Most Arcanist states actually had a fair bit of liberalism growing.
Even today we still live and die by the city-state format the Arcanists set in stone.
Obviously this is an abridged version, but when the Coast War broke out at the end of the 300s...
Skip forward a few decades. Suddenly you have to have documentation wherever you go.
Citizenship became a curse or a blessing, depending on which one you had- and when.
Like, most people just think about the Coast War as this huge apocalyptic event where ninety percent of humans died.
But that didn't really happen until the real fighting in the 470s.
It was six decades of paranoia, escalating tensions, local conflicts, proxy wars, and mago-industrial madness.
The market didn't go to war. The invisible hand of the market would have preferred to live in peace.
I'm sure the war was very profitable for those industrialists.
Free Trade. The Freestriders made that declaration as a response to this period of isolationism, I assume.
Tsk. That's an Azgalist talking point.
Sure, lots of money to be made crafting war machines, armor, weapons.
But what happens when you run out of poor fools to wear them?
What happens when the factories go empty?
And even worse: what happens when those powerful Arcanists begin seizing the means of productions to fuel their war machines even further?
There's no profit to be found when Mister Wizard comes to take your factory.
Slowly at first, and then quickly, the Arcanists stopped everyone from moving about.
The cities that focused on necromancy even ran out of bodies to animate. Could you imagine?
The entire system that let these fucking Arcanists sit on their thrones - and the hard working capitalists that helped them build it - all of it, gone.
Some people say it was 'weakening magic' that threw down those idiots.
Ask the people who saw it in Reeds, the ones who survived, and they'll tell you...
The Arcanists lost because they assumed they alone were responsible for the greatness of Old Diminor.
When in reality, it was all capitalism, baby!
Yeah. In 481 AE you got the Fall of Thazen, as the final nail in the almost figurative coffin.
Within a year, the ruins were covered in gold-yellow flags.
Those heroic and oppressed industrialists had rallied the people to fight for universal suffrage.
They exchanged one set of corrupt leaders for another.
Yeah, yeah. You've read all the Azgalist propaganda as well.
Comparing capitalists to Arcanists is like comparing a hot stove to a Fireball.
One can be mishandled, sure. The other will leave you with third degree burns all over your body. It's like, not even comparable.
Just cook a nice meal and shut up!
You did spend a year or so being obsessed with Reedsian literature on the topic.
Point is, the Invisible Hand of the Market is guiding us. And it'll keep guiding us towards a world that's a hell of a lot more liberal and a hell of a lot more prosperous.
Just as long as you avoid falling into some Azgalist propaganda hole again.
This tale you have heard so many times.
A modern myth. As the old keep being stripped away.
Freestriderism. An Unstoppable Force, charging across the Coast.
You must arm yourself against the storm. Quickly. Before you are crushed between the waves.
There are a hundred different ideologies with a thousand different interpretations and branches.
Out of all of them? Reedsian Freestriderism is probably the most powerful at the moment.
You shouldn't throw all your eggs in the proverbial bag of holding, but it certainly won't hurt to move a bit closer underneath that yellow banner.

So if you've got all of these forces pushing you in all kinds of directions- maybe try being a bit of an Immovable Object? One that's sitting squarely in the good graces of your new Reedsian overlords...
Seriously. If even the apolitical boogeyman can agree to lean Freestrider, you really can't go wrong, can you?
Let's pick one of these. (Choose a feat.)
Bear arms. Fight against the forces of the world.
Until the end of time itself.
Unstoppable Force - Alters ability score modifiers on WEAPONS. Positive modifiers get +1 and negative modifiers get -1.
You just gotta reach out and... grab!
Invisible Hand - Advantage on all trifle checks.
A convenient cooperation. With no real downsides to it.
Let those Freestrider impulses guide your purchasing decisions...
Immovable Object - Your Dexterity modifier also adds to your haggle discount in shops, stacking with Charisma.
Remember to think like a capitalist. Which means, choose whatever works best for you.
Stand tall, against the eastern torrent.
Soon enough, you'll get that sweet, sweet ownership over everything you ever wanted.
This will be a highly productive collaboration.
Oh yes. As long as you stop it with that fence-sitting bullshit. Now go! Buy low, sell high!

You already know everything there is to know about this paper.
You look at the wrinkled piece of paper.
Its content half-legible through grime and stains.
However- there is something about this ranting text that could prove useful.
You squint at the faded text.
Most of it is mundane. Supply lists. Coordinates. Complaints.
But at the bottom, something different.
A passage written in another hand.
The text shifts into formal script.
'Dominus noster mox redibit, posteri eius insidiantur.'
'Magnus Thesaurus Esuthi intra muros sedis amissae iacet, sub pedibus proditorum.'
'Spiritum filii eius primogeniti inveni et cum eo locutus sum.'
'Ossa eius captiva in illis arcanis aulis tenentur, praesentia spurcae ducis goblinorum stabilita.'
'Quamdiu illa terram illam tenet, divinae reliquiae eius non movebuntur - sed ingressum obsignavi.'
The translation forms in your mind, from what you remember from your days of Towers courses.
You'd need magic to translate it properly.
You cast the spell, touching the paper.
The words shift in your mind.
'Our master will soon return, his descendants lay in wait.'
'The great Trove of Esuth sits within the walls of the lost seat, under the feet of the traitors.'
'I have found and spoken with the spirit of his first-born son.'
'His bones are caught in those arcane halls, kept stable by the presence of a filthy goblin chieftess.'
'As long as she holds that ground, his divine remnants will not move - but I have sealed the entrance.'
'It will only open for a true follower.'
'Hail, Esuth! May Fire Sweep the Coast!'
The Trove of Esuth. A legendary hoard.
These adventurers were hunting for it.
This paper might be the key.
Or at least part of it.
The Trove of Esuth... worth investigating.
Worshipping dead gods tends to make people theatrical.
You already know everything there is to know about this paper.

Worshipping dead gods tends to make people theatrical.
Push over the line- destroying each worthless monster in the way.
That's how you get ahead in life.
Aggressive competence, with a stoic demeanor.
...What? Where is this thought coming from?
That's how I always do it.
As the adrenaline wears off - as the world comes back into perfect, regular clarity after such a deeply euphoric fight - you're standing here, realizing...
This is where you're supposed to be. The most competent man around.
The fact that I'm alive is the most impressive part of all of this.
I would love to never fight another troll in my life.
It is also the number one sign of your supremacy.
This taste of pure near-instant-bloodmisted death has got you hooked.
Empires falling and kingdoms rising. Men with goodness in their hearts bearing the burden of reigniting spirits, breaking darkness, smashing evil.
You have finally reached that point.
The point of actually feeling like a Professional Cleric.
So all I need to do in order to feel like I'm good at my job is kill an extremely dangerous (and probably now extinct) gigantic monster?
The romantic ideal of heroism, knights, and kings is fascist propaganda, used to elicit unrealistic aesthetic ideals for young men, radicalizing them towards nationalistic agendas.
...How long is the average life expectancy for cleric again?
Of course. I won't ask again.
The greatest job in the world.
And what is wrong with that?
It's discriminatory to appeal to human men.
It makes them feel bad about the world when they get stuck working in the Lamplighter's Guild, thus incentivizing violence against the system.

Nationalism promotes terrible, hateful ideas. We don't want to be feudal states of old. Nor do we want a repeat of the Arcane Era madness. We want a liberal, modern man. One who is nice, and calm, and loving, and never hurts anybody ever.
Why should you not be allowed to dream in the modern world?
Because I am dangerous. Only women and non-humans should be allowed to exist in the future.
I'm allowed to dream. But only in bite-sized, moderate ways, as to not scare the less fortunate.
We had our chance, and we made the Coast War happen. Now it's time for someone else to take the reins.
Violence is not always the answer- but if there is no capability of violence, then what stops the system from walking all over you?
If democracy is your saving grace, what's stopping the system from removing that as well?
There is nothing admirable about a man incapable of force.
In an ideal modern city-life, you wouldn't need force.
It is in such a utopia that manly force would especially need to be honed.
There is a self-destructive hatred festering within men. Within you.
Some might say it is driven by the forces of various powers in the world. Some might say it is inherent to this Post-War Era.
But it does not help anyone.
A man is not inherently evil for desiring greatness. Nor is he mad for throwing himself into danger.
There is always a need for men to take and rule and protect.
What is needed, is a way for men to dream freely, to aim towards the ultimate greatness of the self - aimed at helping everyone around.
So they do not become thieves or tyrants or ruthless gatekeepers.
If there are no positive outlets for these desires, then that is exactly where they will end up.
A generation of men told to be calm and nice will grow up to be violent and wild. A generation of men told to be COMPETENT and GOOD will grow up to be wise kings and lovable people.
I see the point. But I think it's more complex than that.
Then you need to spend more time understanding yourself.
Or you will continue down this false road of femininity.
You're the one who thinks it. Every day. Every night.
Feels like you might be leaning in the wrong direction here.
The goal isn't to break yourself for some illusionary audience.
The larger that creature is, the more you should be getting paid.
Not in troves or loot- that shit's a nice bonus, but you gotta get this on paper before you dive in.
If you're ever fighting a troll again, you'll be doing so for a maximized hourly rate with every possible gruesome death calculated into that final price.
Your life on the line? That pouch is getting fat. Them's the rules.
I've heard about clerics who go into freelancing. Could be cool.
Sure. But like, way more lucrative.
Can you even say that with a straight face?
Clerics are in high demand, especially these days. Clerics with your kind of esoteric know-how?
Right. I'm not sure this is for me.
I do like getting paid a livable wage.
You know for damn sure that you're just gonna keep throwing yourself into danger like this. So GET PAID for it, dumbass!
OH yes. The Towers are infamous for their tight budgets after all.
Taking the gamble for pay is good, sure.
But to actually live to tell the tale? For that, you need more than luck.
You need the Luck of the Fool.
Stupid is in the eye of the beholder.
Every time you head down here it's a gamble. Every time you take on a job - and there will surely be even more ruthless jobs than that troll in the future - you will roll the die.
That die being a fifty-fifty chance of death and destruction. What did the red one call it?
That's it. That's what you're risking every time.
So embrace it. Focus on being really, really lucky.
You have survived long enough on gut and feeling and ultimately pointless gambles.
It is time to calculate your odds. Properly.
As you can clearly evaluate, each time you take on a challenge, your outcomes are quite extreme.
It appears you are just as likely to succeed in an unlikely and extreme manner, as much as you commonly find yourself in a radical and rather terrible failure.
I don't agree with that assessment.
Even by recognizing your personal skill level involved in any particular task, the overwhelming power of CHANCE is evidently your clearest ally.
Instead, let us lean upon the Strings for balance. A tightly strung balance, one borne out of a beautifully Curved, Yet Balanced Bell.
If you before had an equal chance of each outcome, which is quite volatile, this would instead allow you to take control of the outcome, aligning each attempt around the center- the average.
This would reliably increase your odds at succeeding at tasks you are equipped to handle, while dramatically decreasing your failure rate at those you are not.
I'm not entirely sure what this means.
In short: the bell curve allows you to rely more on your own abilities, rather than random chance.
Of course, this would impact both you, and your opponents.
Jor's Strings are universal, after all.
Tell me more about Luck of the Fool.
Tell me more about Curved, Yet Balanced Bell.
I'm ready to make a decision. (Choose a feat.)
If you're gonna gamble, might as well save yourself the biggest fails.
Luck of the Fool - Whenever you roll a natural 1, you get another result and take 1d4 damage. This effect can repeat to infinity. This damage cannot take you below 1 HP.
You've dodged enough shit to get down here.
In the future you'll be ready. For anything!
Dungeoneering Reflexes - Whenever you roll 10 or below, add an extra proficiency to the roll.
This is it. You feel it.
Professional Cleric - You cannot critically fail.
Naturally, you should only take on challenges you are capable of succeeding at.
This will lower any risks involved.
Curved, Yet Balanced Bell - Whenever any d20 is rolled, the Strings of Jor causes the outcome to lean towards 10. The chance of critical rolls are diminished.
These are all tools of your trade.
Each will help you in some way. Your choice here is to focus on one path.
The gamble. The delve. The veteran. Or the thinker.
You'll be very happy about this the next time you're about to stub your toe.
You know how to act in this world.
Now, until the day you die.

Push over the line- destroying each worthless monster in the way.

The dragons took us, as we were the ideal followers. Powerful. Loyal. Honorable.
Stolen from our homeland. Now we rule.
I wish I was a slave to a big dragon...
I have no opinion of the enslavement of humans.
That is a euphemism of ill intent.
A rotten tale, with a glorious end.
We had no sovereignty. No makt of our own.
DO not even utter such gull-shit!
That is falsely proven by several layers of ironic thumping debates in your skull.
Flesh to work their fields and fill their hoards.
We were delivered via gates. Each of us from a different world, with different myths, varying skin colors, unique cultures - all of us human.
This united us. This was our strength, against those filthy lizards.
Today, it is imperative we do not lose that sovereignty. The makt, which has been constructed tirelessly over the course of centuries.
That, which was ruthlessly ruined... by the Arcanists.
The Arcanists brought us to greater heights than ever before. They made humans the most powerful people on Jor.
We lost everything, all thanks to them.
I wish I was an Arcanist...
I have no opinion on the destruction of ninety percent of the human population. I'm apolitical.
Humans made themselves powerful. It was the corruption of the Arcanists that brought it all down.
You are foolish to believe such magocratic propaganda.
You wish for the idea of sovereignty. Nothing else.
It was the GREATEST TRAGEDY, ever to strike the Coast.
Doug Doulin Dim shoved his blade into the heart of Tisereldanour. From that moment on, mankind relished in their dominance of Old Diminor.
Man built kingdoms. Man received the blessings of multiple gods. More gods than most folk had ever seen in written history.
Man followed wise philosopher kings. In that, we grew strong.
It was not until half a millennia later - after half a century of mago-conflict - that it all crumbled into dust.
But you descend from those few who disavowed the carnage.
You descend from Urthfolk. Those purehearted men who listened to Urth's promise of a free kingdom in Askan, and rode the waves west.
Those were the men who built the great old. Those are the men who carry humanity to modernity, following their good, pure, strong, and eternally wise, god-king.
I wasn't even alive during his time.
We will carry his legacy. It is our duty.
Yet your body holds the blood, flesh, and spirit of his legacy.
Your body refutes that statement. On a purely instinctual level.
His spirit lives on in you. In your flesh. Your SOUL.
Forever. Until the end of everything.
What about the humans who remained in Old Diminor? Or Reeds, I mean.
So Norvik is the only human state left in the world.
What are the Freestriders doing in Norvik then?
After waves of weaponized plagues, cursebombs, and rogue construct armies, only small parts of a few enclaves survived.
Those scattered humans banded together to reform city-states in the rubble.
But the Freestrider menace rose alongside them.
But Freestriderism is a human idea, isn't it?
That is what they want you to think.
In order to refill the ranks of empty industries, the so-called liberators of the Free Cities opened all the borders. They made citizenship a commodity. They invited everyone into their fallen lands.
Within a few decades, humans became a minority in Reeds.
The acreage not permanently ruined by magic were repurposed by folk from around the Coast, as well as those barbaric creatures living on the borderlands.
Ask any man in the east, and he will tell you: My people have no state. We are stateless.
Why is that a bad thing?
No one likes being a minority.
That doesn't matter in a multicultural society.
If one does not care about sovereignty, no.
In a democratic governship - a rule by the masses, and their cultural ideals and thoughts - it is the minority who suffer and the majority who conquers.
As humans lost their majority in Reeds, the Free Cities, those humans left also lost control of their own destiny. Freestriders seek prosperity, yes, but at the cost of sovereignty.
In the broader Coastal community, at least.
There might be hidden enclaves in Hae'Xi and Ym you have read about. The Japanese are legendary after all. But in effect, yes.
You are the last of humanity. Keep that in mind.
One blade at a time. Sometimes a tiny cut, a simple graze. But now, as their election looms, you can feel it.
A plunge, deep into your flesh.
The last piece of humanity left in the realm.
Yes. Think it through. Everything inside yourself, that which makes you a man, tells you that...
It is worth it. The Freestriders are making the world better. We don't need to be a majority.
We don't deserve to be a majority. We gave that up when we destroyed ourselves in the Coast War.
We need to retain a majority. That's the only way we can ensure control of our own destiny.
Who cares what your neighbors look like? What matters is how much money you can make by importing as many-
We must nourish a new type of man.
One that is loving and kind, and welcoming to everyone that is different. We must-
You Azgal fool. This is a debate of the body. The mind should be left in the gutters, along with the rest of its rotting crevices filled with degenerate propaganda.
Urth built a land for mankind to rule. To govern. To hold as their own.
Everyone else is a visitor or a guest. We, the human people, are its owners.
As such, we must hold our ground. We must bear that weight. We must Defend the City!
You think you're some True Norvikian? You're not even fully human, you hairy fuck!
The VAST MAJORITY of your FLESH is URTHFOLK, to the <shake>BONE</shake>!
Then there's a tiny part honorable dwarf, yes.
So that's okay, then? Just as long as it's a statistically negligent amount of foreign blood.
Sure, whatever. You wanna be anti-Freestrider? Go ahead. Might as well just-
Each vein points in the direction of greatness.
Each limb, an extension of something untold.
As is forecalled by eras and sagas of old:
Chaos lies in the nature of man. Only kept in check by the state, which tendrils the land.
Your uneasy stomach rumbles at the thought of the world outside your control.
You must make things right. The only way you know: to sit atop the monster.
Tell me more about Defend the City.
Protect the innocent. Be the hero you were meant to be.
Defend the City - Alters ability score modifiers on HELMETS. Positive modifiers get +1 and negative modifiers get -1.
Yeah, not sure exactly what this masturbatory nationalistic fantasy is all about.
But sure! Be a pure-blooded human if you really want to. Go flex in a damn mirror or whatever.
True Norvikian - Roll initiative with Strength, if it is higher than your Dexterity.
Take hold of the beast that is the realm.
Leviathan Apparatus - Enemies always roll initiative with disadvantage.

In the Coinlord's garden you encounter a porcelain specter.
Her armor glimmers. Her very presence blinding you.
Polished armor. Inhuman. Her presence is both ethereal and demanding.
She floats gently in the air, strangely unaffected by the river winds.
Your goblin companion keeps his distance, muttering under his breath a word of disdain.
The creature does not turn to face you. Instead her many eyes simply pivot their gaze.
A handful of celestials are employed by the city, after the Clanlands Deal of '13.
The flowers of the Coinlord's garden dance beneath her.
You will be one of them.
She replies by bowing her head slightly, whilst her many eyes glare at you.
She bows her head slightly, whilst her many eyes glare at you.
In other words, somebody told her you'd be coming here.
Then she opens her mouth to speak, in a projected cold tone...
She says her name like a declaration of existence.
Not really an introduction, more of a checklist of etiquette.
As she states her name you realize her mouth is not moving.
She is in your head? A parasite...
The goblin gives you an uncomfortable look.
He clearly does not enjoy having telepathic conversations like this.
Snell is joining the mind talk? Great.
The angel's many eyes stare at you as you speak with your animated rag.
You can talk with your scarf if you want to. It's your right. Probably.
She just stares at you, with many, many eyes.
You feel the eyes watching you.
Speaking with this ethereal creature is unnerving.
Whatever you do, don't mention the eyes.
You should mention it. The eyes. She'll appreciate it.
Oh. You're feeling something here alright.
You could shake her up with a nice comment.
YES. Stand tall. Tell her how you feel.
That is what a MAN would do.
She is too powerful. The angel would step upon your soul. Stomp on it.
Her domination would be good and just. Let it happen.
ROLL8 cha-Tell her what you think of her many eyes.
The smooth thoughts and endless love.
This has gone too far. The only way out is...
As you finish your rambling, there is a silence sweeping over the Magistrate's yard.
As your helm touches the ground of the Magister's garden, you feel the urge - the overwhelming and excruciating final urge - to tell this angel one more thing.
The goblin's eyes dart back and forth between the two of you.
The words escape your lips like smooth... uh... honey?
Yeah. Honey. I'm sure she loves that.
She looks down on you. With many eyes.
No response at first, but then she raises her head.
A dry reply. At least she's not pissed.
The words formulate in your mind and get quickly tangled.
Your goblin companion quickly grabs your hand and pulls you back.
Your muscles strain by sheer fear. You cannot begin to reach such divinity.
Snell gives you a crazed look.
The goblin thinks you are actually insane.
After a short pause, the angel appears to lift her head.
The word cuts harsh through your bellows.
Eh. Probably not worth it. But still.
Cut to the chase. She clearly has no interest in small talk.
Maybe do some small talk. Then ask the big questions.
Glorious. Your flesh can take the heat.
The angel is most likely a radiant member of the Golden Horde.
Her many eyes look upwards for a moment. Then back to you.
The eyes just stare at you.
Some of her eyes glance towards the door to your left. Then back to you.
Great. Nice. We got that cleared up.
It's like talking to a brick wall!
We're getting nowhere with this. Be direct with her.
That would make things easier. Just... don't be too rude. Okay?
She is a messenger of divinity. Overwhelming to the senses.
Slowly the angel turns her 'face' towards you for the first time.
Your comment causes the angel to turn her 'face' towards you for the first time.
Your outburst causes the angel to turn her 'face' towards you for the first time.
Oh gods. You're a dead man.
Walk away. Walk away. Walk away.
A common derogatory saying among the Horde - it means something along the lines of: 'the little one speaks'.
She is mocking you. For sure.
Why is everyone in this damn district mocking you? You're The Cleric, they should treat you with respect!
You can sense she does not see you as worthy. Is that alright with you?
Perhaps... you should stand up for yourself.
As you do, she makes her first real move.
Her hand placed upon the top of your helmet.
She's fast! You barely saw it...!
The goblin grits his teeth and stumbles back.
The ring rotates around her head and now yours. She is close.
She leans in. You see through the gap in her skull where her eyes should be.
Before taking a step back, leaving you frozen.
A glorious sacrifice. A fray. An END. How far a woman can bring a man, to see... to feel his end upon their meeting. This is how you will die, if things come to pass.
Your body is p-u-l-l-e-d towards her.
Despite her harsh words, you see her body language relax. Maybe.
It's hard to tell. But she does not appear truly angry.
You catch her head twitching to the left.
She looks to her right, towards the building leaning against the ancient tower.
After a moment of silence she says, \
Ah. She means, after midnight. So between 00:00 and 06:00.
It's good that you managed to solve this difficult code. The angel speaks in riddles.
He will only be available after midnight. Between 00:00 and 06:00.
Several of her eyes look to the entryway to her right.
And whoever is attempting to make it one, deserves a life of suffering.
'Restouir' is one of those fancy terms imported from anuran culture. Though it doesn't mean anything in anuran, they kind of got it wrong.
You can sense her inner feelings about this term are something akin to 'pure unadulterated rage.'
She is clearly a master manipulator, just standing there. Staring.
She says only what is needed. That is commendable.
The angel greets you with silence, her many eyes gazing in your direction.
(Just stare at her many, many eyes.)
The angel slowly floats away from you.
Leaving you behind on the top of Tolstad.
She hands you a rusty key along with a note.
The angel only stares at you with many eyes.
Then she turns and floats away.
(Look at the key in your hand.)
It's a key to the Guard Tower.
The one in front of you.
Your goblin companion glances over, and then towards the building.
(Look at the note in your other hand.)
This will allow you entrance to the tea shop.
No one would ever speak ill of that wonderful spice.
.Tea_Shop_Seen==0-Time to head into the tea shop then.
.Tea_Shop_Seen==1-But I already made it inside the tea shop?
Yes. It is already quite late.
This would have made the process much easier.
Don't lie. You're no good at it.
She does not respond to your question.
Either she does not know or cannot say.
Her eyes focus in on you. \
Her eyes spin gently around her head.
She knows a lot about this quest of yours...
You didn't actually think this living tank of a creature would just open up to you, right?
Semantics. Like potatoes and that other vegetable.
Snell chuckles, then goes back to being awfully quiet.
He is so afraid of the angel he stifles his witty remarks.
The common tongue of man is called Diminorian. Of Old Diminor.
It's normal language. You know, the one normal people use.
Her many eyes stare at you in silence.
The goblin glances at the celestial and shuts his mouth.
Never heard that before? Perhaps you should make it more clear to them.
She says it like it's a foreign delicacy.
She opens her mouth and lets out a sound.
A laugh? You must be entertaining her.
Oh. She's laughing. She's actually entertained by your madness.
A slight nervous laugh leaves the goblin's maw.
She most likely said something akin to, I do indeed. Don't you, goblin?
Wait... does Snell not speak Goblin? Wow.
Few goblins speak Goblin in Norvik after the integration efforts.
Not integration. It was a genocide. The Goblin Genocide.
In the Coinlord's garden you encounter a porcelain specter.
Her armor glimmers. Her very presence blinding you.
Polished armor. Inhuman. Her presence is both ethereal and demanding.
She floats gently in the air, strangely unaffected by the river winds.
Your goblin companion keeps his distance, muttering under his breath a word of disdain.
The creature does not turn to face you. Instead her many eyes simply pivot their gaze.
A handful of celestials are employed by the city, after the Clanlands Deal of '13.
The flowers of the Coinlord's garden dance beneath her.
You will be one of them.
She replies by bowing her head slightly, whilst her many eyes glare at you.
She bows her head slightly, whilst her many eyes glare at you.
In other words, somebody told her you'd be coming here.
Then she opens her mouth to speak, in a projected cold tone...
She says her name like a declaration of existence.
Not really an introduction, more of a checklist of etiquette.
As she states her name you realize her mouth is not moving.
She is in your head? A parasite...
The goblin gives you an uncomfortable look.
He clearly does not enjoy having telepathic conversations like this.
Snell is joining the mind talk? Great.
The angel's many eyes stare at you as you speak with your animated rag.
You can talk with your scarf if you want to. It's your right. Probably.
She just stares at you, with many, many eyes.
You feel the eyes watching you.
Speaking with this ethereal creature is unnerving.
Whatever you do, don't mention the eyes.
You should mention it. The eyes. She'll appreciate it.
Oh. You're feeling something here alright.
You could shake her up with a nice comment.
YES. Stand tall. Tell her how you feel.
That is what a MAN would do.
She is too powerful. The angel would step upon your soul. Stomp on it.
Her domination would be good and just. Let it happen.
ROLL8 cha-Tell her what you think of her many eyes.
The smooth thoughts and endless love.
This has gone too far. The only way out is...
As you finish your rambling, there is a silence sweeping over the Magistrate's yard.
As your helm touches the ground of the Magister's garden, you feel the urge - the overwhelming and excruciating final urge - to tell this angel one more thing.
The goblin's eyes dart back and forth between the two of you.
The words escape your lips like smooth... uh... honey?
Yeah. Honey. I'm sure she loves that.
She looks down on you. With many eyes.
No response at first, but then she raises her head.
A dry reply. At least she's not pissed.

The kid behind the counter taps a pile of newspapers. \
Ah. The Post. Yes, that is a good magazine.
The Post is a respectable magazine. A Postie is a good nickname.
A punishment wrought in corporate greed. If the worker is punished for the temperament of the customer, then the duty of protection - along with all costs involving that - also falls upon the worker.
The business owner should be the one forced to pay that price. To deliver a secure working environment, before they are allowed to take out any profits.
Great. Make it even harder for the entrepreneur to survive the chaos of the world. That'll help.

He quickly stammers out something else as he hands over the newspaper. \
He quickly grabs three coins from under the counter and tosses them to you.
Taking bribes from children? What are you, a monster?
Taking bribes from kids? Cool. Get them hooked young.
Everyone should pay for your services. In fact, they should worship you.
The ridiculous nature of the fact that you gave him a bribe, and now he's bribing you back, is not lost on you.
.TS_Arn_Bribe==1-(Smile as you put the three coins in your pouch.) \
He's thinking of how to explain this to you.
The kid glances around the corner of the shop. Then he shrugs. \
But he's clearly a hard worker. Fucking over the business would fuck him over as well.
This kid is making an enormous profit for his boss each day, probably.
Arn stares at your outstretched hand. He glances back and forth for a moment before a smile spreads on his face.
Arn grabs your hand and shakes it.
You manipulated this kid like he was a pile of wet clay.
The Freestriders shall be eradicated with interactions such as this, one at a time.
The boy is scared of the egalitarian dream.
Good. As all humans should be.
He stops himself and looks down.
The kid is a radical in hiding.
He dreams of Urth returning every night on the roofs of Norvik. He is a child of the city, for the city.
That's one way of doing it.
He cares about politics a lot, actually.
The kid grabs a copy of The Post and starts reading.
Sad. Would've been more fun if he grabbed News in Norvik. That one's mostly rumors and sensationalist bullshit. It's great.
These polls are mainly done in rich areas. You know. The human areas.
Your goblin companion stares at you.
He stares at the bottom of the page.
He looks up from his news-piracy and gives you a dumbfounded look.
The kid starts flipping the rest of the pages.
Jokes on him. You don't, uh... know how to read.
(Look over your shoulder, towards the tea shop across the street.)
The kid gestures a large sphere for you.
He is trying his best to remember.
He's just a kid. Shouldn't have to deal with such terrible things.
He's strong. He should take pride in that.
He's trying to avoid the subject, to not cause you to become more aggressive.
His eyes shot open for a split second.
He's lying. Just look at his eyes move around like that.
He looks nervous. It's probably because he has nothing to tell you, and feels bad about it.
The kid likes money. Give him some and he'll sing.
ROLL10 cha-Convince him to tell you. Through the power of social manipulation.
He means it as a 'are you really giving me that much money for some silly information?'
.CROWNS>=10-(Clear your throat and hand him nine additional crowns.) \
.CROWNS<=0-(Realize you actually don't have a crown, and you just slid forward some lint from your pouch.)
Then he gladly pockets the coins and gives you a nervous smile.
He looks at you with his eyes.
...What else would he look at you with?
NICE. Never let them expect your next move.
Be like a wind. A wind that loves money.
(Slide it in a circle around the counter.)
He stares at the coin as you playfully move it around the wooden surface.
The kid grabs it with a smile.
The kid stares at the stuff you placed on the counter.
By all that's holy and unholy...
The kid shrugs, and dusts it off the counter.
The power of manipulation flows through you.
Because you know exactly what to tell this young lad.
Ask him. What was the fake name?
Street kiosk kid, swearing like a sailor.
And you're not one of them. Not today.
But... what do I do then? He's clearly hiding something.
Maybe scream and slam your fist down on the counter?
But whatever you do, do NOT scream and slam your fist down on the counter.
(Scream and slam your fist on the counter.)
(Do not scream, and then slam your fist down on the counter.)
As you do, the kid jumps back.
You feel your fist grow numb as you lift it off the counter.
You idiot! Quick, explain it away. Tell him you saw a bug or something.
The slam frightens him, and he jumps back.
The kid nervously glances to the side as you get more and more agitated.
You truly completed this quest like a HERO.
You're lucky no one got killed.
(Don't respond, just look for an escape route.)
The kid makes sure it's not damaged before turning back to you with a scowl.
The kid looks around for help.
He appears to realize the person who's supposed to help him in this situation is currently attempting a robbery.
You must perform... actions... or... die...
Fucking hell, just- JUST RUN AWAY!
You're not meant to be alive like this.
You need evidence. The magazines are the key...
Your goblin companion sees your shivering and muttering.
The kid stares at you, open-mouthed. \
You wrap the length of your arms around a row of magazines and papers.
You press them together into a clump.
This ain't very smooth, gotta say.
Turning to leave you feel your heart clench.
The pressure of being a master-thief has caught up to you.
You've surely got their attention now.
As you do, you see the kid glaring at you over the counter.
Snell is helping place back some of the magazines you hurriedly ripped from the stand.
The goblin gives you a kick.
You've caused quite the mess, haven't you?
Consequences like these hurt your soul.
Not because of some personal or reputational injury.
But simply because of the hurt to the community this represents.
The kid just keeps picking up papers.
He's not the type to complain.
At least not to your face.
You get down on your knees.
It takes a quarter for all the papers to be back in fashion.
By the end the kid just gives you a quick nod.
Maybe wait a bit until you try to steal from the kiosk again.
You get your breathing under control.
It's clear what you should do in this tense situation.
Your feet guide you away from the kiosk.

The scent of something acidic hits you, when the girl tosses cuts of a red fruit into the cauldron.
You have not seen those fruits before. They must be some type of Undercoast delicacy.
Those fruits - they're some type of mutated lingonberry.
She points towards a bowl on your left. Up on the shelf. It has purple flowers in it.
There's another bowl next to it, full of what looks like bat-wings.
DC11 dex-(Hand her the bowl of bat-wings.)
She cackles, and takes a few steps from the cauldron and grabs the bowl herself.
The goblin leans in, hissing out, \
The hag keeps stirring her giant wooden spoon in the cauldron's goop.
She scoffs, and nods her head towards the Goblin.
She lets out a cackling laugh.
Betraying her age. That's a crone's laugh if you ever heard one.
The hagling gives you an esoteric look for approximately seven seconds.
The hag leans in over her cauldron.
.DS_SmarterDone==1-(Point towards the dancing goblin in a cage on her shelf.) \
The hag stares at you for several seconds.
That's the way to do it. Smooth.
Your companion turns to you and hisses, \
It's this new generation. They can't handle the banter.
A free spoon is a free spoon. Maybe you could sell it at that market next week?
Please stop being weird now and actually try to act in an arbitrary way that makes her like you more, instead of the opposite.
You have not hurt her, as much as you have bewildered her with your strange behavior.
Great answer. That means, please take it from me.
This is a waste of time.
She leans in over the cauldron. \
Taking a deep breath, she then glares at you. \
As a hag and diviner, she could very well have access to high tier spells.
She scoffs, giving you an incredulous look.
Then she averts her gaze slowly enough for it to look comedic.
Your companion gives you a look and a shrug. \
She shrugs and gazes into the slits in your helmet. \
Truth. She truly did not do anything.
Her eyes are stuck on the cauldron. She does not lift her gaze.
After a few seconds, she opens her mouth, whispering, \
Snell gives you a solemn look, before mustering up the courage to say something in the silence.
She quickly closes her mouth. A flood of emotions. \
A twinge of pain in her heart.
You read a micro-expression: she regrets trying for that simile.
The hag gives you a wicked eyeing. \
Lisa glances at a pouch on the table.
She lets out a short laugh. \
A promise, which her soul does not keep.
The goblin gives her a tired groan.
She stares at you, in silence.
The girl folds her arms and then averts her gaze.
You can feel the age vanish from her as she pleads with you.
This is no hag. It is but a young girl caught in trouble.
The hagling sighs dramatically over her brew.
She's not faking this. She's just scared of getting caught up in some bullshit, it seems.

The room grows quiet as Lisa keeps stirring her pot.
The heart pumps blood to all veins.
A fully interconnected series of stops, each necessary for survival of the whole.
All stops must accept that blood. They can never be cut or clogged, or the entire system collapses in an instant. This is the weakness of the egalitarian dream. As well as its strength.
Unity in spirit. At both rise and fall.
That sounds like an awfully risky system to live in.
Azgalism was born under the conditions of twos.
The first: a literacy of the masses, created in various forms across different cultures during the Arcane Era.
The second: a deep enough divide in wealth between the masses and their masters.
Feudalism holds deep and fast due to the inability for its serfs to understand the functions of their system. To see their own shackles, and how to break them.

The more advanced governmental structures of both federated magedoms, parliamentary republics, and constitutional empires all grow partly due to their bourgeoisifying nature, and modern city-state industrial developments.

In this, some states hold out better than others. Some floating cities in Old Diminor, specifically that of the Shadow Lord, would grant access to magical food to all the lower classes. Others would offer free education for all children, no matter their birth, in order to find those gifted in the art of the esoteric. Actions like these, along with a steadily rising quality of life, prolonged the spread of working class resentment.

However, Røykfall Empire had no such luck. The kingly line had sat on the Forgethrone for hundreds of years. Their monarchical, autocratic ways were roughly burrowed into every cavern and fungus farm. And their population...
Through developments of the Arcane Era, trickling down through the cracks of the earth - the already massive dwarven populace exploded.
Within a generation, it doubled. Approximately thirty million dwarves lived in the Empire by 440 AE. The vast majority of them living in absolute poverty.
Starvation was rampant in times of rot and conflict. Heavy taxation mixed with little to no protection from monsters or raids, meant that life for the serfs was hard and rough.
It was in these conditions a man was born and raised, his dreams filled with one thing: emancipation. The right of every dwarf to his own hammer.
So the dwarves had been pressure cooked in wealth inequality for too long, while also having access to information about better systems.
The birth of Azgalism. What a story.
And it had been spoken of for decades.
It technically goes back even further.
Every tale with a purpose is a form of propaganda. And this one goes back even further.
What Azgal did, was gain access to books by banned dwarven scholars, gnomish thinkers, anuran philosophers - all pointing him towards his own brand of a radical egalitarian ideology.

Azgalism exploded with Azgal and his revolution, yes. But it was born as early as the Divine Era, for these dreams had lingered in the minds of all folk for as long as there have been shackles, and they have been documented about for as long as there has been paper to write on.
Yet it took a walking god to push the oppressive regime back.
So Azgal was a god, then?
Azgal wasn't a god. He was a man.
He wasn't a god, but he was a... walking god?
A man, blessed by a divine force.
He was no god. Yet it is true, despite what many say, that he used great esoteric power and strength to lead his revolution.
Understandable. The Empire probably had a ton of wizards and magical forces, just like the Arcanists during that Era. The Coast War was underway, after all.
A horrible truth, but truth nonetheless.
Many dwarves died during the initial fighting. Many more died during the first years of the AFF.
They died for a good cause.
That's a lot of dead dwarves.
The exact number is hard to tell.
This is a point of contention for you, and many others.
The dwarves quickly pulled out of the Coast War, leaving the VK behind. But with that, many dwarves were put back into the work force and reunited with their families after decades of warfare.

Economically, they were unable to manage such a sprawling set of states directly. But the fungus farms and vast cavern valleys were handed to the people who worked on those lands, instead of the greedy nobles who once lorded over them all.
Azgal took steps in the right direction. Even as all the forces in the world pushed against him.
The monarchies were awful, but his system killed too many people.
He should have left the monarchies as they were. They would modernize in time, anyhow. Just like the rest of the surface kingdoms did.
But the lessons learned were taken to heart.
Some might believe that. However, looking at Old Diminor, it should be clear that no man will ever give up power out of the goodness of his heart.
If not for the AFF itself, but then for the dreams it sprung around the Coast.
Today, Azgalism in all its forms, along with the lessons of the Folkhem, show us a brave new path.
That of a softer Azgalism. That of democratic socialism.
Just as bad as pure Azgalism, but with a dangerous allure of normalcy.
We have had enough of those.
That is your greed sneaking through.
The argument against social democracy in Azgal circles is simple, however.
The forces of capital easily consume and co-opt the socialist goals - they did just this in Savalni, before it was taken in by the Freestriders as a Free City.
Norvik itself is under that same threat.
As such, it remains a debate. The dwarves who have made a great cooperation with the humans of the city all have converted to this nicer form of slow, incrementalism. A social-democratic ideal.

In this Unity between Humans and Dwarves, you also see this grander vision of an egalitarian future. For there is no greater example of post-folktype-exclusivism. But at the same time, the younger more hotheaded Azgalists also have a dream of their own: one of truly owning the means of production posthaste, like Azgal intended.
So the question remains. You have dealt with it first-hand in the past...
Should Norvik embrace radical Azgalism or continue down the path of social democracy?
We need radical change, or the Freestriders will win.
We need incrementalism, else humans will reject Azgalism for good.
We need to avoid Azgalism as much as possible. If nothing else, to just avoid reliving the starvation of the Folkhem.
DC11 int-We need someone to lead us forward. One supreme leader. Someone like Azgal, but more magocratic.
DC17 con-We need to all be farmers. Every. Single. One. Of. Us. (Let the dirt flow in your veins.)
We need to argue among ourselves for another decade or so before we're able to do anything productive, I think.
We need to be apolitical, and just let the forces of the world live and let live. That'll work great.
Think what you will, say what you can- but the Azgalists have at the very least mastered the modern pathways of communication.
The copper press. Those mad Azgalists are utilizing it just as they should: to influence the minds of the populace.
During better times, the Arcanists would do as such in more direct ways of course, but the irony of that is lost upon these poor fools.
If anything, you can learn something from watching their Mass Media Propaganda in motion.
Sharing of ideas is very different from mind control.
You would think that. However, in statistical analysis, the difference is all the smaller than one would assume.
The printing of ideas is the second step towards emancipation. The realization of a better world.
Cast out from below. Exiled from their homeland. Spread across the land. Above and beneath.
Like Agrarians, they seed the earth. Growing. A weed or a fertile wheat - you do not know.
A weed, surely. As such, the land requires a proper cleansing at some point.
Not a weed. A fungus, feeding a family of sixteen dwarvlings all hungry from working the fields. And if they do not have enough to feed themselves - their neighbours will share what they have.
That is the dream. That is the communal spirit of Azgalism.
Tell me more about Mass Media Propaganda.
I'm ready to settle this debate. (Choose a feat.)
If you are not allowed to do it esoterically, then do it via the written word.
Mass Media Propaganda - Alters ability score modifiers on TEXTS. Positive modifiers get +1 and negative modifiers get -1.
A social, communal, Azgalism utopia. It can be built.
Human-Dwarf Unity - While you are at full health, whenever you take damage, those dice are rolled with disadvantage.
Azgalian Endurance - For every 3 hours spent being exhausted, you lose 1 level of exhaustion.
In a future of your magedom, it could be useful to have some propaganda-training.
In a society in which folktype is only a suggestion, and not a barrier of any kind.
Now, you too, will live on.
Standing... the test of time. Like an idea.

Many dwarves died during the initial fighting. Many more died during the first years of the AFF.
They died for a good cause.
That's a lot of dead dwarves.
The exact number is hard to tell.
This is a point of contention for you, and many others.
The dwarves quickly pulled out of the Coast War, leaving the VK behind. But with that, many dwarves were put back into the work force and reunited with their families after decades of warfare.

Economically, they were unable to manage such a sprawling set of states directly. But the fungus farms and vast cavern valleys were handed to the people who worked on those lands, instead of the greedy nobles who once lorded over them all.
Azgal took steps in the right direction. Even as all the forces in the world pushed against him.
The monarchies were awful, but his system killed too many people.
He should have left the monarchies as they were. They would modernize in time, anyhow. Just like the rest of the surface kingdoms did.
But the lessons learned were taken to heart.
Some might believe that. However, looking at Old Diminor, it should be clear that no man will ever give up power out of the goodness of his heart.
If not for the AFF itself, but then for the dreams it sprung around the Coast.
Today, Azgalism in all its forms, along with the lessons of the Folkhem, show us a brave new path.
That of a softer Azgalism. That of democratic socialism.
Just as bad as pure Azgalism, but with a dangerous allure of normalcy.
We have had enough of those.
That is your greed sneaking through.
The argument against social democracy in Azgal circles is simple, however.
The forces of capital easily consume and co-opt the socialist goals - they did just this in Savalni, before it was taken in by the Freestriders as a Free City.
Norvik itself is under that same threat.
As such, it remains a debate. The dwarves who have made a great cooperation with the humans of the city all have converted to this nicer form of slow, incrementalism. A social-democratic ideal.

In this Unity between Humans and Dwarves, you also see this grander vision of an egalitarian future. For there is no greater example of post-folktype-exclusivism. But at the same time, the younger more hotheaded Azgalists also have a dream of their own: one of truly owning the means of production posthaste, like Azgal intended.
So the question remains. You have dealt with it first-hand in the past...
Should Norvik embrace radical Azgalism or continue down the path of social democracy?
We need radical change, or the Freestriders will win.
We need incrementalism, else humans will reject Azgalism for good.
We need to avoid Azgalism as much as possible. If nothing else, to just avoid reliving the starvation of the Folkhem.
DC11 int-We need someone to lead us forward. One supreme leader. Someone like Azgal, but more magocratic.
DC17 con-We need to all be farmers. Every. Single. One. Of. Us. (Let the dirt flow in your veins.)
We need to argue among ourselves for another decade or so before we're able to do anything productive, I think.
We need to be apolitical, and just let the forces of the world live and let live. That'll work great.
Think what you will, say what you can- but the Azgalists have at the very least mastered the modern pathways of communication.
The copper press. Those mad Azgalists are utilizing it just as they should: to influence the minds of the populace.
During better times, the Arcanists would do as such in more direct ways of course, but the irony of that is lost upon these poor fools.
If anything, you can learn something from watching their Mass Media Propaganda in motion.
Sharing of ideas is very different from mind control.
You would think that. However, in statistical analysis, the difference is all the smaller than one would assume.
The printing of ideas is the second step towards emancipation. The realization of a better world.
Cast out from below. Exiled from their homeland. Spread across the land. Above and beneath.
Like Agrarians, they seed the earth. Growing. A weed or a fertile wheat - you do not know.
A weed, surely. As such, the land requires a proper cleansing at some point.
Not a weed. A fungus, feeding a family of sixteen dwarvlings all hungry from working the fields. And if they do not have enough to feed themselves - their neighbours will share what they have.
That is the dream. That is the communal spirit of Azgalism.
Tell me more about Mass Media Propaganda.
I'm ready to settle this debate. (Choose a feat.)
If you are not allowed to do it esoterically, then do it via the written word.
Mass Media Propaganda - Alters ability score modifiers on TEXTS. Positive modifiers get +1 and negative modifiers get -1.
A social, communal, Azgalism utopia. It can be built.
Human-Dwarf Unity - While you are at full health, whenever you take damage, those dice are rolled with disadvantage.
Azgalian Endurance - For every 3 hours spent being exhausted, you lose 1 level of exhaustion.
In a future of your magedom, it could be useful to have some propaganda-training.
In a society in which folktype is only a suggestion, and not a barrier of any kind.
Now, you too, will live on.
Standing... the test of time. Like an idea.
Stepping in front of the petrified person-statue, you...
As if someone is listening in.
It's difficult not to. You're very loud.
As if literally no one was listening to you. In any way.
Don't mean to intrude, but that's not really correct, buddy.
Why are you thinking in a squeaky little voice?
That voice must be some kind of telepathy. Find the source.
Stop thinking so loud, it's giving me a headache.
Your eyes land upon the nose of the stone statue. At the very tip, you see something.
We might be tiny, but trust us, we've got a bite!
Freaking great to meet with you, buddy.
Your palm hits the nose of the statue perfectly, removing the strange voice from your skull.
The creature was clearly a monster of some kind.
It was good to strike first.
.Snell==0-A good slap. (Wipe your glove on the statue.)
You do so. The ant was too small to leave any sort of mark.
You slap the statue with an open hand.
Don't call me such mean things.
As you strike your palm once again, this time you feel something push against it.
One of your fingers bends backwards, and cracks.
Okay, fine, but... don't try to kill me, then?!
Look... let's just calm down here.
We're not a bad guy. Not a threat.
And sorry about the finger. Self-defense, right?
That's a definitional question. So let's move on.
If you'd hit, I'd be dead. So... we're even?
You can call us ONE. We didn't really used to have a name, since we're a hive mind.
So ONE will do for now.
Yeah, good to meet you too.
Right now, yeah. It's weird to be without the hive.
The goblin stares at you. Then stares at the statue.
.Snell==1-(Point at Snell.) Explain this to my companion so he doesn't think I'm crazy, please.
He gives you a worried look.
(Point at Snell.) Talk to my companion as well, ant.
So... what do you want, ant?
Right. Sup, dude. You a goblin?
Snell perks up and looks around.
He gets ready to smash it.
We're... alone, and it's just... we just need to ask for a favor!
Yeah, yeah. It was a whole thing.
But we left! You won! So let bygones be... by the <shake>gones</shake>. As they say.
He stares at the ant for a few seconds. Then he frowns. \
We were driven from the area a while ago, but... we left me behind.
This whole identity thing is really new to me. I've never been disconnected from the hive for this long before.
You don't want to be an individual? I think it's pretty great.
Being alone sucks. Rejoining the hive sounds like a good idea. You should do that.
I wonder if I could join a hive...
Hm. I haven't really thought about it too much.
But sincerely... that just sounds like a thing for you non-hivers.
With that uniform, it looks like you already have!
But if you help me out... how about we give you something in return?
I don't need a reward. I'll help you because it's the correct thing to do.
You can still get the reward, since you're such a nice guy.
And also, we don't really have any use for it.
I have a positive inclination towards quests.
I'm the questing kind of guy.
Actually yes. I'll help you out.
No. I do not want to help you out.
Ah. Too bad. But if you change your mind...
We know the location of a secret stash! It's buried in the Undercoast Path, like, below... this place.
Sure is. The Undercoast Path is... well it leads all the way down to the Undercoast.
Right past the Gravity Flip. Although you won't need to go that far down. It's too dangerous for someone like you anyhow.
A single cranium ant is not dangerous. Their psionic strength is counted en masse. A thousand of these are required to do anything beyond normal ant-sized power moves.
Beware. The ant might be small, but it is still psionic.
You must help this creature. It is asking for your help, and it would really not be that much of a hassle.
You're a real cool guy, buddy.
We'll be sure to remember you as one of the good ones. Once I get back, that is.
Questing time. You should go down below to the Undercoast Path, and then talk to the ant.
Just... pick him out of your pocket when it's time. Or something like that.
Thanks. (Put the ant in your pocket and leave.)
The ant looks at you from the nose of the statue.
The ant crawls around in your hand.
You need to head down. To the Undercoast Path. That's where you'll be able to drop me off.
You can drop me off. Right here.
.ITEMLOOTED_CB_Cranium_Ant_Cranium_Ant==0-(Pick up the ant.) I will get you to your brothers, my friend.
Aren't you afraid of getting, you know, crushed? Since you're so tiny?
What are you voting in the election?
What do you know about the tea shop?
.MeriIntro==1-The man in yellow... that's Meriadoc, right?
My telekinesis isn't too powerful going solo, but it's enough to keep myself defended from boots and any crevices I might fall into.
But don't get used to it.
You don't have a name in your hive?
Must be nice. To be a part of something larger.
Only nice to be a part of a hive if you're the queen. Or king.
It sounds like your kind's way of trying to emulate our hive-mindness.
You know, everyone's getting to voice their opinions leading to a unified direction.
Except you get to think for yourself.
Being in a hive-mind would certainly make the whole process a lot easier.
Hm. Suppose that ain't too bad...
Although it sounds inefficient as all hells.
But suppose that's the only way for your kind to join together in that way, eh?
Although, I imagine it's... a bit different if you actually get to have an individual mind on things.
You know, think about it yourself. By yourself. With yourself.
Yes, good. The ant might have picked up something interesting about the explosion.
Oh, so that's what that was the other day.
The ant clearly doesn't know anything interesting.
Hey! That's not fair. I'm real perceptive, you know!
The ant must know something you don't. Ask him harder!
There was some stuff happening after the shaking, I'll admit.
A human in yellow clothing went past the Roots. He did something esoteric to the hairy brute to get in.
I assumed he'd come back screaming from the south tunnels.
I'll let you know if I find out something new, I guess.
Oh, that guy over by the pillar? Yeah. That's the guy!
The tiny ant jumps off your glove, and starts sprinting off.
Hold on, what about my reward?
The ant stops and turns around.
You did well. Didn't expect much, to be honest.
But... I can feel the hive nearby. We're... already stronger.
Sure. But you mentioned a treasure...
An image flashes through your skull.
The sudden visuals burn in, leaving a mark.
Here. It's underneath that thing you found me on.
Just check under that crack. You'll get some goodies we hid before they drove us off.
A crack in the statue- the exact place where you found this tiny creature.
It's our special little gift from us to you, buddy.
Hold on, is this stuff you've stolen from us?
I'm not sure this is yours to hand out, buddy.
This is a treasure we collected far before you greenskins ventured into the tunnels.
Is that image in my head going to go away soon?
The ant rotates around a bit on the spot.
It's been nice to follow you around for a bit, buddy. I've enjoyed it.
Are you sure you want to rejoin the hive?
If you ever have some time off, feel free to visit.
Go to your hive. You've been away for too long, One.
The ant moves a bit more erratically.
Yes. I do kind of sort of enjoy this whole... thinking-for-yourself-thing.
It's annoying sometimes. And it's weird to have your mind by yourself all the time.
But... most importantly, I'm thinking... maybe I could have a choice in who I share my thoughts with, instead of just sharing it per default. You know?
So what are you going to do, then?
Let me... let me join you for a bit longer. Just in your pocket. If that's alright.
You have a knack for making friends, don't you?
No. You need to make your own way.
He jumps back into your pocket.
Great! Honestly, I really want to see what this whole election thing is all about...
The ant nods. A very tiny nod.
You're right. I can't rely on you or the hive.
That's not something we have, really. But sure!
All this individualistic behavior is so annoying.
You see him wave a very tiny hand in your direction.
We will be One, once again.
He turns and heads off, disappearing among straws of alien grass and cracked ruins.
WHERE HAVE YOU TAKEN ME, HUMAN?!
You could enter the Pillar too?
WHAT DO YOU MEAN YOU FORGOT?!
What Pillar? We were in your pocket!
This is not, I- we- we are not feeling ANYTHING here!
That sounds like a good thing.
Focus on getting us out of here! Now!
We- I'll stick around for a bit longer, if that's alright.
I've got some ideas of what to do next. But first I want to check out that election you've got going on...
Everyone seems to be very excited for it. I share in this excitement.
This election thing is looking way cool!
So many people thinking about... so much stuff.
I wonder what... I should vote for...?
Stepping in front of the petrified person-statue, you...
As if someone is listening in.
It's difficult not to. You're very loud.
As if literally no one was listening to you. In any way.
Don't mean to intrude, but that's not really correct, buddy.

This election thing is looking way cool!
So many people thinking about... so much stuff.
I wonder what... I should vote for...?
You hold up the paper - finding it to actually be a torn-out page of a book. The name in the margin suggests the book is called 'A Gnome's Starter Guide to Esoteric Memorization'.
The goblin leans in over your shoulder and reads it as well.
'The first step to understanding esoteric memorization is understanding the underlying powers at play.'
'Jor's Strings stretch across his Coast, invisible to the eye, yet connected to all things living and dead.'
'A spell is simply a set of rules you must follow in order to manipulate those Strings into an esoteric effect.'
'Yet those rules are themselves esoteric in nature. As such, most wizards will find it impossible to remember more than a handful of spells at a time.'
He turns back to look at the page.
'This is the big divide between cantrips and actual spells, as cantrips are both easy to remember and will not consume a spell slot.'
'Spell slots are the common representation of how many spells of each spell level a caster is able to perform without rest.'
'As a Gnome, you are naturally gifted in the art of illusion magic. Other schools will be more difficult to memorize.'
'A suggested harmless spell to use in training is the first level spell GREASE. To increase your chances at memorization, attempt to cast it at least two times per day.'
In the margins there's a hand-written hard-to-read note.
In the margins there's a hand-written note, that you manage to spot. It's written hastily and bold, like the author really wanted to hype themselves up.
It says, 'EVERY EVENING, AND EVERY LUNCH BREAK AT WORK. REMEMBER!! YOU CAN DO IT!'
You deduce that this did not pan out well for the author.
Oh, magnificent deduction. You truly are a genius.
The goblin leans out over the ridge, looking down into the river.
How mysterious. You should try to figure out what happened here.
An esoteric mishap. Magic is a blade without a handle, and so on.
FC10 int-Hmm... let's see. There's a grease-stain on the bridge...
DC10 int-Indeed. Grease is a dangerous spell.
It must mean something. But what?
ROLL7 int-Figure out what happened here.
The gnome cast grease, and then slipped off the side of the bridge.
That explains it. Poor, poor gnome.
The grease must have caused a chain reaction of esoteric energies.
An esoteric pocket must have been born, for just a split second, with some terrible rule.
Something like, 'All gnomes are turned to dust'. Or 'life does not exist when near magical grease'.
The poor gnome slipped on his grease patch and fell.
The esoteric pocket seems more plausible.
A greasy ending. Went out like he went in.
What are you speaking of? Is that some sort of innuendo?
Eh. Wasn't a great one, but yeah.
Well don't do that. It's dirty.
You quickly memorize the piece of paper.
