You quickly memorize the piece of paper.
The runes fill your senses as you approach.
This ancient shrine appears suitable for you.
It's just a little figurine. But this counts as a shrine.
The Toulin'catl were just as obsessed as modern humans with servitude.
Only they worshiped their host, Jor himself.
The wooden collection of... stuff is supposed to be a shrine to Urth.
At the center you see his red eye. Watching over you. Yet...
The color is faded. This monument is hollow, void of all life and divinity.
And worse... it's covered in rotten apples.
.Undercoast_Path==1-I've seriously never interacted with a shrine until now? All throughout this adventure of mine?
.Guard_Tower==1-Why is this the first shrine I encounter? Aren't they supposed to be everywhere?
.Lower_Lair==1-A sacred shrine, left in this state? It's an insult to Urth and all of his people.
These old things are still around, huh?
It is a wonder you are still alive.
You simply must open your eyes.
You've been walking past them all day.
You need to prepare your spells. You haven't done it all day.
The green and blue agree on something?
Yet you are very much aware that this is not the worst you'll see.
You'll have to fight hard to keep Urth's dream afloat.
This city is lost. But it is not yet lost.
You're also aware this city is very much in need of change.
Change from what? An honorable lifestyle? A peaceful city for humans to prosper in?
Change from stagnation and a mire of decay.
Urth's Crusade brought triumph. The corruption brought after his fall is due to exactly that kind of filthy rhetoric.
You're certainly allowed to be wrong in your opinion there.
DC10 dex-Stop complaining. It won't solve anything.
This is giving me a headache.
True. But you must stay vigilant.
Do not let Freestriderism destroy your way of life.
More like, don't stand in the way of your people's prosperity.
It does not take long for you to move some of the nasty apples away from the front of the shrine.
A small improvement. But big in the heart.
Might as well. Leave the whole thing to rot!
A remnant of blindness and decay.
Hard to let go of old national pride.
But progress will always lead the way!
It is a shrine to your god. Protect it.
No. You will find shrines like this all over Norvik.
There are many gods, listening to our prayers. Always.
Just remember that they might not respond so clearly.
DC11 wis-So I can pray to some random god?
DC8 str-Urth. I need to pray to Urth.
So it's a waste of time?
You may pray as you wish.
There's an internal element to it that is underappreciated.
To cast spells, you must first prepare them at shrines.
Prepared spells can be cast when the cleric has a reason to do so, either in or out of dialogs. Each cast expends one spell slot of the corresponding level.
And yes, this little figurine does still count as a shrine.
Shouldn't I move it? It's like, in the middle of the road.
.SHRINE_PrayerofHealing==0-Wait, can't I heal here? Through divine assurance or something?
The figure of Urth. This is a make-shift shrine - meant to stand exactly where it is, and where it might go.
Ready to serve whoever needs him the most.
You wish you were a man capable of something similar.
Staring at the chiseled monument you focus your mind.
You take a deep breath and begin your prayer.
Can I return here, when I have more things to think about?
You let your mind go through the basics of what you know.
The tea shop was destroyed in the middle of the day.
Your goal is to enter the tea shop and investigate it properly.
What sort of business did he conduct there?
Her reach sharp. Her gaze strong.
She wants you to investigate the tea shop and report to her.
The content of that report will determine much.
A powerful man with much to lose.
If he fails his grip, there is no reason to appease him.
If he retains it, you are wise to stay his ire.
Speak with them. Learn their stories.
Pass judgment if you so wish. They do not care.
The Gold always shines in the end.
A woman. A warrior. A divine creature.
She is here for no other reason than her own broken ways.
Listen to her, and you will be rewarded.
If you so wish to carry her blessing.
But what you do inside will change everything.
She is at the center of a web.
But who was right? And who was wrong?
Your vote will be the most important of all.
Ask those around you what they will vote for and you might find your own voice along the way.
But both sides want what's best for their city.
He was last seen here, on Tolstad.
If you find him, you will surely be rewarded.
An ancient creature who guards a door to nothing.
You are not the first to complete it.
If you do, it will unveil something great.
But the answer is not in dialog.
You will have to cast magic to show your prowess.
It's everything, from faction to home life to street.
What you choose might be a first step.
For they are not yours to have.
Though there might be some who fancy you.
If you show yourself to be a good man.
Or bad. If you want the baddies.
The Undercoast elf is a good woman.
She holds many natural friends in this unnatural place.
If you wish to seek the green path, then be nice to her, and her friends.
Perhaps one day you will join her.
But you feel no worry. For the danger is but a misunderstanding, in your mind.
The answer is deep. You know it all too well.
They do not tell you the whole truth.
And there are deals in the background you are not privy to.
Tread carefully around the pale man and his four helpers.
It was not an accident. You are not so clumsy.
But the question here is not why you fell.
The question is: why do you live?
You must be a lonely guy.
You are the only one who is always there.
No one ever answers your prayer.
DC14 int-These are not actually gods, are they?
Urth is dead. Dead gods do not speak.
Dead, yes. But he speaks through you.
You've devoted your life to follow in his footsteps.
Like a father you're never able to surpass. Or something like that.
Illym the Mender was an elven goddess. Builder of an army, a cult, and a way of life in Askanii.
A real lady, this Mender. You've heard the stories. Beautiful. Terrifying. All the elf-stereotypes rolled into one.
They say a look from her could turn a man's heart until his dying breath.
Though most men have a story like that, so it's probably more a woman-thing than an elf goddess-thing.
She would not respond, even if you had something to tell her.
She's mostly known around these parts as that crazy witch that broke in and charmed a hundred warriors back in the day.
It's a classic tale. But Urth had a nice chat with her, and everything was fine.
Urth and Illym stayed away from each other's business. Officially, at least.
She died a couple of decades before Urth did. Nowadays the elves are a bit more... expansionistic.
Azgal Ashmask was a dwarven god of exiles and egalitarianism.
His followers do not pray to him, for he is not even seen as a god by most.
A weakling who sacrificed his people for an unattainable foolish dream.
Though there are parts of his tale you cannot deny are quite... romantic.
A lone man. Rising against impossible odds.
No wonder his followers still rampage across Askanii.
A trickster. The type of god only famous for his ridiculous ploys.
Tokig the Gambler is often seen as a silly goblin myth.
In truth, there are few goblins who still worship him after the conquest.
Praying to Tokig would, if you for some reason would get a response, only result in the exact opposite of whatever you wanted.
Lady Fair and Those Not is the founding myth of the Fordnip Commonwealth. A young halfling blessed with the strength to defeat an army, inspiring the survival of her clan. However...
The halfling deity of Askan! But she only had that divine spark for a few days before being slain.
It's said she led five thousand halflings through the snowy hills filled with foul foes.
I'm afraid you're not too familiar with the Fordnip's goddess, nor how to pray to her.
First of many - a Beast of Jor.
Boulba was that of the hunt.
Behemoths who raged across the Coast.
In his name we celebrate the eternal hunt.
These are the common divine avatars known to the various folk of Norvik.
Long ago the true outsider-gods invaded JOR's Realm. When the gates shut, their divine essence awakened walking gods along the Coast.
The gods you know are but a spiritual vessel of the True Gods beyond our Realm.
Yet they are the only gods you know. Albeit faintly, they listen to your prayers and grant divine power to their clerics.
In short: gods weren't allowed on Jor's turf - so they sent subsidiaries.
It's like one big scheme of influence. Absolute genius.
A ruse played by Powers in the Beyond.
The gods do not usually grant divine interventions for minor injuries.
Possibly? The gods are kind to you.
If you want healing, you have to do it yourself.
With the power channeled through Urth? Yes.
Come on. Is it really channeled? The guy's dead. Faded. Gone.
You're most likely simply handling some esoteric leftovers, smudged on the Strings.
Of course you can heal here. But only if you pray hard enough!
ROLL16 wis-Attempt a Prayer of Healing.
You focus your mind on Urth's symbol. His red eye.
You're filled with a spirit of nostalgia and power.
Sadly, you are unable to cast second level spells at this time.
But it won't be long until you will be.
You recite a few words of remembrance, before you sense the channeled Strings around the shrine.
Sure is. But you sense there is something you've missed.
What were those words? The ones you learned at the Towers?
The runes fill your senses as you approach.
This ancient shrine appears suitable for you.
It's just a little figurine. But this counts as a shrine.
The Toulin'catl were just as obsessed as modern humans with servitude.
Only they worshiped their host, Jor himself.
The wooden collection of... stuff is supposed to be a shrine to Urth.
At the center you see his red eye. Watching over you. Yet...
The color is faded. This monument is hollow, void of all life and divinity.
And worse... it's covered in rotten apples.

Behind the counter stands a woman of fiendish origin. Focused on filling up one of the taps, she immediately stops when she notices your approach.
She gives you her full attention.
This woman is a perfect hub of information. Pick her brain.
Leave her brain alone. Violence is never the answer.
You should not use such violent expressions anyhow.
She pictures something. It is not pretty.
We all share this place. It is as much hers as it is yours.
She laughs and sticks a sharp finger in your face. \
The two of them clearly share a past - in an enemies-to-lovers kind of way. Only without the lovers part.
The fiendish tavern-keep suddenly stops cleaning the counter, and stares at you.
She tosses the cloth over her shoulder.
She's almost desperate to spill the beans.
She gives you a demonic smirk.
There's tension here... you should mention it.
There's tension here... if you want a rise out of him, ask about it.
She laughs and leans in over the counter.
She gives you a stern frown.
The goblin closes his eyes and mutters something to himself.
Your companion doesn't seem to enjoy the horned woman's attention.
You do not feel any nervousness speaking to this woman.
She is speaking in sexual undertones, yet... you do not feel weak.
Well, duh. She's not your type.
Who wouldn't? Powerful scaly ladies cannot be beat.
In fact, they should be respected. Worshiped.
You do not feel even an ounce of fear or trepidation before this woman.
She is not sexually compatible with you.
Imagine partnering with such a blabbermouth?
It would be your own personal hell.
You do not feel that way about her.
She pumps a fist in the air.
She is claiming to be one hundred twenty-five percent demon.
She gives your companion an expression that lands somewhere between 'cute puppy' and 'tigress.'
You feel a deep respect for the goblin grow in the lower part of your groin.
That might be something you ate.
She gives you a quick shrug. \
She leans in with a smirk. \
She gestures drumming on the counter.
She shakes her head and repeats her answer. \
Idiotic. You'd have to survive for weeks in a tiny cramped space.
Some bird-folk can survive for months without sustenance.
And if he had friends on board, they could just let him out after they passed the immigration control.
That's a wicked good plan. You should look for him on Waterlane. At a warehouse, or something.
She bursts into a deep chuckle. \
Don't just say that shit. It's sensitive information!
You hear a groan coming from Snell.
She looks around, her eyes wide open.
She's making sure no one else heard.
She tries to keep herself from talking too loudly, but it's clearly difficult.
You see the gears turning behind her bright eyes.
You've given her the most valuable asset on the Coast: juicy info.
This might bite you in the ass later.
Your question causes her to let out a hearty laugh.
She probably gets hundreds of people using that fake name during any given week.
She slams a hand onto the counter.
Meek perks up at this, eyeing the counter with sudden interest.
She smiles just a bit too much.
Her smile is fake when before it was genuine.
She avoided your question like a convo queen.
The awkwardness. You feel your bones shivering.
Best method, just give her some silence. She'll keep talking.
It takes approximately three and a half seconds for her to confess: \
She's playing games. Time to play along!
What the fuck does that mean?
.DS_RixTip==0-(Toss ten crowns into the tipping jar.)
.DS_RixTip2==0-(Toss a hundred crowns into the tipping jar.)
.DS_RixTip3==0-(Toss ONE THOUSAND crowns right across the counter.)
ROLL20 cha-Convince her to tell you about the hag.
The fiendish woman furrows her brow.
Then she gives you a quiet laugh. \
She taps the tipping jar on the counter.
She scrambles to collect it all in her apron.
Her maw opens in wild laughs.
We're talking about an emotional bond here. A deep bond.
But she's also kind of tired of it.
She won't sacrifice too much for it.
So just... go ahead and convince her that you'll help.
That you'll clean up all this mess.
She looks down behind the counter, biting her lower lip.
You hear her let out a deep sigh.
You've got no clue why the hell she would hide this information from you.
You've gotta say something to her. She's waiting for you to try.
Slam your fist into the counter and yell, \
She jumps at your sudden violent act.
A fancy nobleman in armor. Got it.
A clear sign of corruption. What sort of man would choose to show skin instead of iron?
She gives you a deflated look. \
She puffs up her chest and gives you a proud nod. \
She gives you a shocked expression with a confused smile. \
She gives you a deep chuckle. \
That's terrible! Someone should do something.
She sighs and rolls her eyes. \
She remembers the Boulgorn Blood Cult. Remnants of a minor but powerful god that grew during the Coast War.
They had an offshoot in Norvik when you were little. You heard about them a lot as the Urthguard fought day and night to eliminate them under the city.
The goblin mutters something under his breath.
She leans forward over the counter and stares at you.
Your companion looks at her, and then back to you.
She gives you a serious expression and nods.
She looks confused for a moment, before continuing.
(Press a hand against your cheek and scream.) \
She's taken aback by your exclamation.
She clearly loves to talk about her own thoughts and opinions for days, so...
You've made this woman very happy.
She holds up a hand and gives you an incredulous look.
She smiles with her whole face.
She enthusiastically meets your hand in a satisfying smack.
Oh, stop that. Fascism is objectively bad and screaming it at the top of your lungs is a good thing.
You do not mean 'fuck fascism' when you say it. You mean 'fuck the city'. Your city.
You've got problems if you think that. Which you do, so...
This woman is overly familiar for a living. Admirable!
She's giving a chance to those who often cannot make their own wage. Wonderful.
She's exploiting women who have been left behind by their communities. Awful.
She must be making a shit-ton of coin. Good for her!
She nods her head in direction of the massive sphinx at the end of the chamber.
Our lord saw this divine being and allowed her to continue her sacred mission.
To convince a Sphinx - a creature sent by the very high gods - to alter her life's mission... this woman must be highly persuasive.
This fiend loves the sound of her own voice. Could be very useful.
That means she can vote, but she can't hold office.
She does not like talking about this too much, which is why she stops herself from ranting anymore than she already has.
Your legs falter. Such a blow...
If you won't listen to your senses, then at least listen to the bartender.
She's hitting on you! Hit back!
She's too close. Tell her to back off.
Not really the moment for that.
She would destroy you, despite being the same size as you.
Nope. Not your type. You need someone... stronger.
Impossible. You do not find her attractive.
(The red one is correct, but still. Boring.)
It's hard to put into words. But whatever you're looking for, this horned woman does not have *it*.
Too big of a question to ask while you're awkwardly staring at this horned woman.
It's mean to reduce a person to however horny they might appear.
Whatever- just say *something*. You've been staring at her silently for like fifteen seconds now.
Eh. Just say something. You've been staring at her silently for like fifteen seconds now.
She shows her sharp teeth in a bright smirk.
Devils are lawful evil. Demons are chaotic evil.
They're both creatures of evil, flawed to the core.
Stare at her for a bit, then say, \

So just... go ahead and convince her that you'll help.
That you'll clean up all this mess.
She looks down behind the counter, biting her lower lip.
You hear her let out a deep sigh.
You've got no clue why the hell she would hide this information from you.
You've gotta say something to her. She's waiting for you to try.
Slam your fist into the counter and yell, \
She jumps at your sudden violent act.
A fancy nobleman in armor. Got it.
A clear sign of corruption. What sort of man would choose to show skin instead of iron?
She gives you a deflated look. \
She puffs up her chest and gives you a proud nod. \
She gives you a shocked expression with a confused smile. \
She gives you a deep chuckle. \
That's terrible! Someone should do something.
She sighs and rolls her eyes. \
She remembers the Boulgorn Blood Cult. Remnants of a minor but powerful god that grew during the Coast War.
They had an offshoot in Norvik when you were little. You heard about them a lot as the Urthguard fought day and night to eliminate them under the city.
The goblin mutters something under his breath.
She leans forward over the counter and stares at you.
Your companion looks at her, and then back to you.
She gives you a serious expression and nods.
She looks confused for a moment, before continuing.
(Press a hand against your cheek and scream.) \
She's taken aback by your exclamation.
She clearly loves to talk about her own thoughts and opinions for days, so...
You've made this woman very happy.
She holds up a hand and gives you an incredulous look.
She smiles with her whole face.
She enthusiastically meets your hand in a satisfying smack.
Oh, stop that. Fascism is objectively bad and screaming it at the top of your lungs is a good thing.
You do not mean 'fuck fascism' when you say it. You mean 'fuck the city'. Your city.
You've got problems if you think that. Which you do, so...
This woman is overly familiar for a living. Admirable!
She's giving a chance to those who often cannot make their own wage. Wonderful.
She's exploiting women who have been left behind by their communities. Awful.
She must be making a shit-ton of coin. Good for her!
She nods her head in direction of the massive sphinx at the end of the chamber.
Our lord saw this divine being and allowed her to continue her sacred mission.
To convince a Sphinx - a creature sent by the very high gods - to alter her life's mission... this woman must be highly persuasive.
This fiend loves the sound of her own voice. Could be very useful.
That means she can vote, but she can't hold office.
She does not like talking about this too much, which is why she stops herself from ranting anymore than she already has.
Your legs falter. Such a blow...
If you won't listen to your senses, then at least listen to the bartender.
She's hitting on you! Hit back!
She's too close. Tell her to back off.
Not really the moment for that.
She would destroy you, despite being the same size as you.
Nope. Not your type. You need someone... stronger.
Impossible. You do not find her attractive.
(The red one is correct, but still. Boring.)
It's hard to put into words. But whatever you're looking for, this horned woman does not have *it*.
Too big of a question to ask while you're awkwardly staring at this horned woman.
It's mean to reduce a person to however horny they might appear.
Whatever- just say *something*. You've been staring at her silently for like fifteen seconds now.
Eh. Just say something. You've been staring at her silently for like fifteen seconds now.
She shows her sharp teeth in a bright smirk.
Devils are lawful evil. Demons are chaotic evil.
They're both creatures of evil, flawed to the core.

You are unaffected by the wink.
Her usual smile turns into a scowl.
Then she returns to a wide, inviting grin.
She slaps your hand as it reaches behind the counter.
She puffs up her chest and gives you a demonic glare.
Young women running around the streets. Slinging Azgalist propaganda to unsuspecting citizens.
Boys causing chaos in the name of some hapless, magazine-inspired rebellion.
No it cannot. Too much chaos has been caused by these damn kids.
It can be tolerated. It's just some kids.
The youth are attempting to get political. They're learning as they go.
What? The city will be fine. Don't worry about it.
That kind of looseness will bring order to its knees. It's all-
Indeed. This must be solved. With the might of the state, we shall-
The Azgal Youths did nothing to warrant such push-back.
This Era is one of communication. Ideas, interchanged freely between a populace of free-minded thinkers. Folk dreaming of an egalitarian potential.
However- the problem lies with the unfortunates who break and sear folkarian thought - with reckless unkindness.
You must pray for them. Beg of them.
To please, please, stop being so mean.
...Do you really think that would work?
Appealing to their better nature? Great idea. (Be sarcastic.)
Appealing to their better nature? Great idea. (Don't be sarcastic.)
To believe in the better nature of these boys will not hurt.
We must keep hope for those with anger in their hearts.
Those boys might be unkempt and rough - but they are proud citizens of a great city.
They are performing a service- albeit a crude one. One beyond the realm of law.
But the Youth Azgals? They are even more unlawful. To a much steeper degree.
How do you calculate that degree of unlawfulness?
That's true. In a battle between lawfulness and disorder, those book-burning boys clearly come out on top.
The Azgal girls were just doing a technically criminal crime. The boys were doing multiple literal actual fire-related crimes.
Entirely through the morality of their behavior.
You are FEELING like the boys are in the right.
That is not an unreasonable statement.
It is a morality issue, not a legality issue.
You weigh morality upon the shoulders of ideology in this case. You see that, do you not?
The only shoulders being weighed upon here are your manly, bulging muscular traps.
What has happened here is a complication upon society; a representation of the wider culture.

Young men are drawn towards the rough, downtrodden world of the dreamers. The hope of national unity through an Urthian story. One of leadership and honor. But without that guiding star, there is no such singularity. No control. No wise king to bend the knee to.
Instead, those boys are simply expressing pain through violence. Enacted onto those who are attempting to craft a more caring future.

They, as opposed to young men, are drawn towards a more social type of unity. With welfare and Azgalian flattened hierarchical structures. Beyond Azgal himself (who is by his function also a male figure), it is to womanly people you shall look towards.
Yes. Lady Fair and Those Not. The walking god who gave everything to save her people.
Her words are few, but spread far across the Coast. Her church is quite active among Norvikian society, as an organization of charity. One open to all folk, no matter their origin.
An egalitarian dream. This is why women, as harsh as it is to say, are simply more suited for Norvikian leadership. Lady Fair makes it clear:
A house held by woman stands any season.
I agree. Women clearly build a more thoughtful and caring world for us all. (Feel like a good man for thinking such egalitarian things.)
But I want to lead in the future. And I'm not a woman. Does that mean...?
Good. I have no interest in the future and/or any type of leadership. I want to stay in the darkness and wither away.
I feel like there should be some middle-ground here. What about 'a house held by a person never falls!' - that's catchy, right? Right?
You can be bring wisdom and goodness to the world as a man.
But if a woman rises to the occasion, you must make an effort to enable her rise.
There is no need for that.
You can surely be in a healthy position in the coming flat society.
There is something so powerful about dreams.
Ideas spreading from man to woman. Woman to man.

But the Azgalian dream is not a blessing. It is a smile wrought in pain. A vision of a thousand layers of intersected nets and labels - tangling every person, ensnaring them into oppressor or oppressed.
And there is oppression. There are unfair hierarchies. The world is corrupt.
But not fundamentally. If these boys are pushing back against Azgalism, that is only natural - it is an ideology which pushes a foundational belief that they are, naturally, oppressively superior.

If a young man grows up in squalor, finding zero advantages from him being a man among Patriarchs, nor any power from being a human - especially if he grows up within non-human clusters, something many of the poorest humans do...
When he is unable to find a job at age nineteen, and has almost no political, societal, or economic power in his life...
Which dream do you think he would believe in? The one calling him to fight to reclaim what is his by right: a good life as a Future Patriarch.
Or the one calling him an oppressor?
It does not call him an oppressor. That is an unfair reading.

Azgalism is about systemic change. That young man is trapped in the same cycle of violence that all men find themselves, since the dawn of time: that he must FIGHT. That he must be a PATRIARCH to have that good life.
Azgalism would lay the foundation that allows him to be free from these burdens. He would be free to find that job - that good life - with the same prospects as everyone else.
That foundation is laid by raising women to the forefront. By allowing them to flatten our hierarchies, and bring about a new world.
I suppose if a man already has it pretty terrible in a world where he's supposed to be at top, then a world where his advantages are gone would seem even worse.
I'm sure there's a female perspective here somewhere as well, but I've only really read about that in mom's books.
Seems to me like it's more of a branding issue. The Azgalists need to be more in-tune with the economic aspect of intersectionality. They're clearly losing out on a ton of angry book burning boys.
Look. I just want what's best for women.
No. I don't care about men or women. I am an equal hater. We all suck.
That is, again, the unfair reading, yes.
You can think of it as unfair. Or you can think of it as a clear message.
That is an argument - but it does not change the core values.
DC11 int-Me, as their supreme leader.
Something that I am not smart enough to visualize.
Be more confident. You know this well enough.
That is not a nice way to go through life.
You need to come to that realization...
The one every boy reaches towards the late stages of their capitalistic, pubertistic, and socialistic awakenings, that-
It is only your placement you must understand. Figure out where you've planted these two feet of yours, and accept where your bones will begin their incessantly slow-ish grind into the pavement.
ROLL12 str-Realize your place in the world.
How is this possible? That even being fully awake, your feet feel as though they are floating.
As if you have no tether to the ground nor the world.

Nothing matters. Everything is... loose. And fleeting. Neutered and flat.
There is no real difference in the spirit of men and women. It's all an illusion.
This is far more complicated than you ever could have imagined.
So many relations. So many intersectional divergences.

How a man who feels feminine connects in the hierarchy with a woman who feels womanly but acts manly in order to fit among patriarchal standards - only to be controlled by a manly woman who seeks feminine ideals in order to break those same standards...
It is simply too much for you to make sense of - to break down, to sample even an inch of truth from the whole...
No one said this whole egalitarian dream thing was going to be easy.
But this is... far too much for you. Right now at least.
Maybe it is only your side of the aisle, which you have any hope of understanding this day.
To know man, in order to know woman.
Everyone lay upon Jor's spectrum, each judged for their perceived and internalized values.
You are approximately .52 decimals across the line into masculinity.
That is, in a less abstract definition, a little over halfway to being a full man.
Can I disagree on this, or...?
Opinion are fleeting. Facts are firm.
Does that means I'm a... (Gasp.) ...a woman?!
I'm not sure how to feel about this knowledge.

Fearing it - fearing your position on the spectrum - is weakness by itself.
Of course you can. You're a man. A REAL man.

That makes you .02 more man than the average man.
But I'd prefer to be way manlier.
Halfway to being the most manly, yes.
Let us not get carried away.
Then your first step would be to stop worrying about how manly you are.
You are approximately averagely decently middle-ground manly.
A normally sized manhood inside of a perfectly median-shaped man.
You already have. For many years.
Inside. Your manhood, inside of you.
Manhood is a concept. An idea. A feeling of masculinity, hidden within your body.
You know the truth. So choose a side.
It is... such a simple answer.

As you compare and contrast - as you break away the many specifics and biases and little intricacies that make each man a man and each woman a woman - what is left is but the souls of these folk. These creatures of biological realities and spiritual happenstances.

Each of them loving, and holding, and hating, and screaming. Within Jor's arbitrary spectrum. Within some neatly organized check-boxes on a governmental form. Within the heart and genitalia of each young, virile, beastly person who struts across this artificial landscape.

So you let the bird go.
Gave him his papers and everything.
What, I got a problem with that?

Living up to the Freestrider ideal!

Everyone should be able to come and go as they please. The free market is built upon the ability of the movers of goods - from merchants to sailors to dockworkers - to be able to transfer themselves into situations of required labor.
What about the societal impact of-
Awesome. Yes, the market needs to dictate where and how people live.
So you took the bird's papers.
Made him stick around with the snakes.
What, I got a problem here?
It's time to establish some baseline, okay?
First of all, immigration is good.
Immigration. It's a net positive for a modern society. For Norvik.
Hold on. I don't agree with that-
Yes, it's true. But what's that got to do with-
First of all, I'm apolitical. Second of all-
It's literally the most fundamental policy of the Freestrider bloc.
In the real-old days, the only people with resources to travel would be the aristocrats of societies. In the real-early days, that'd be the top dragon servant. The top slaves.
Then, during the last few centuries that changed. The average person suddenly got the ability to move. On a societal scale, sure. But also in a geographic sense.

Each of the Arcanist splinters dealt with it in different ways. But in general, especially once the Coast War broke out for real, all the borders got real tight. A shoot-on-sight type policy wasn't uncommon.
The Arcanists were terrible, but that doesn't mean nations don't have the right to close their borders.
That was overkill, but we should still have some type of border controls, right?
The powerful should control the weak. Arcanists had it right, as did the kings of old.
Let's go over the facts here.
The number one cause of starvation, poverty, and economically-related suffering is the general lack of prosperity.
Historically, it's understandable why peasants and kings (or early Arcanists) didn't really get this. Because they just didn't have any reference point.
If things were always terrible, you wouldn't even know what 'good' is, right?
Then comes industry. Mass production. Both esoteric stuff - access to free food, healing services, teleportation - and the fundamentals - textile factories, metal refining, and transportation.
All of it giving way to an unprecedented amount of PROSPERITY. For everyone!
It's like cheating in the economy. Everybody gets rich all of a sudden! Even the old aristocrats!
So then you have the question of Nationstates. Of borders.

You can make an argument that freedom of movement goes against certain ideals of nationalism, sure. You can't completely lock your own people away from others. You can't stay out of the global trade. You lose that authoritarian touch.
In return? You get a supra-magical economic boost. One that helps everyone.
You know those Azgals that hate Freestriderism so much? This is the point where they all shut the fuck up and sit down.
Because Azgalism didn't make anything better - not anywhere near the degree that opening the markets did.
And locking down your borders? Not allowing trade, people, labor, goods and services to flow at the behest of the benevolent invisible hand?
That's literally like arbitrarily making your people, all your people, objectively less wealthy (and probably a lot hungrier), just for some dumb ego-trip.
And anyone who disagrees is a fucking idiot.
You have convinced me. I will now allow strangers to come live with me in my apartment.
Yes. Immigration is great and helps us fill out the ranks of the increasingly growing Norvikian industries.
Immigration is amazing, but only for folkarian reasons. We should help people in need by sharing our wealth.
Immigration in balanced amounts is great. But too much and it'll create strife and cultural clashes.
Immigration is a tool of the bourgeois, used to slow down wage increases and dilute working class solidarity.
Immigration is a threat to democracy.

No. Immigration destroys nations. It destroys people. Just because Old Diminor blew up and Reeds took its place as a messed-up coagulation of all the broken survivors, doesn't mean the rest of the Coast has to align with their strange multicultural ideals.
You don't own an apartment. You live with your mom.
I'm trying to be agreeable here.
Come on. Be serious for once. This is hardcore stuff.
This new era is one of constant expansion and increasing value.
We need more people! More workers! More consumers!
Factory owners win. Those poor immigrants win. Everybody wins!
But yeah, guess that's true too.
You get cheap labor. They get to not be oppressed on the other side of the Coast.
Yeah, sure. But the market will take care of that by itself.
The vacant jobs create the immigration appeal by itself. Nobody would move across the world if there aren't any jobs. That would be ridiculous.
What if they move to get away from even worse situations? Wouldn't that overwhelm the nation targeted by the mass immigration?
What if the country offers a good enough life for immigrants that they'll move across the world anyway? Even if there's no economic demand for them?
Wow. Big Azgal-brain-man over here. Using fancy words and everything.
The working class has been systematically lifted out of extreme poverty, all thanks to this oh-so oppressive capital.
After the Coast War, there were two roads: one led to them all turning back into peasants. The other? Incredible industrial prosperity.
You're on that second road now. And the Azgals want to drag you kicking and screaming onto the first.
What? Okay, let's indulge that crazy line-of-thought.
How, exactly, does immigration pose a threat to democracy?
Democracy is a form of sovereignty, allowing the people to govern themselves.

If you change the population, you change the ability for a group of people to govern themselves. Move enough Reedsians to Norvik, and eventually the Urthfolk will lose the ability to use democracy to control their own destiny.
No one is advocating for those kind of radical population changes.
And even if it were to happen, that would only be due to the demand of the market.
Thus, the Urthfolk, while given less political power, will be given such an increase in prosperity that it doesn't matter! Anyone would take that deal!
I'm not sure the peasants of the Divine Era would agree.
Nice talking points. Read them in The Post did ya?
Again, no. This is simply the truth.
Our sovereignty comes first. We are not a subject of the Free Cities.
I am The Cleric of Norvik. Of course I stand by my city, no matter what.
Truth? It's a twisted fucking perspective, that's what it is.
Great. Might as well isolate yourself too. See how far you get without all this FREE TRADE you get from the FREE CITIES and all these FREESTRIDER ships.
The old power will obviously want you to think like this. Scary immigrants sneaking through your ports. People with weird skin colors selling Ukkulian fairy dust to innocent kids.
Traditional values breaking down, all because daddy didn't want to work harder than an unskilled worker fresh from Bloodcity Bay.
It's all bullshit, peddled by idiots with an axe to grind. And it works, clearly.
Emotional, aesthetically obsessed nationalists don't listen to reason. Or numbers. Or logic.
You only crave some impossible ideal of knights fighting dragons and saving damsels.
That the old world sucked. The new world is awesome.

And if we were back in the good old days, you wouldn't be an aristocrat. You'd be a peasant. You'd die at age thirty-three from getting a splinter after working day and night in the fields for scraps.
That's the kind of world Nationalism leads to. And it sucks.
I am not emotional. (Attempt to say it without emoting.)
The reality is this: the human population of this city is not seeing as much of this prosperity as they were promised. People will not tolerate it for much longer.
To rein in the madness of uncontrolled capitalism does not equal going back to feudalism.

Stringmaster - For each level of spell slot you have, you gain one additional maximum spell slot.

Communing Patron - If you take damage that would cause you to go to 0, a mysterious force protects you. If this happens, you take no damage, and lose one 1st level spellslot. If you do not have any 1st level spell slots, this does not happen.
...And yes, this is different from the Godly Voice-thing.
But mostly in aesthetics. Which is the most important aspect, to be fair.
Just... remember to keep your guard up.
The Pillar of Jor, an ancient bulwark. Its Gate rising Beyond.
Yeah, I got pretty deep down to figure this thing out.
I'm still really confused. What did I do exactly?
Yet it continues far, far below.
Each step of the way making it clearer to you.
How little they matter- these vestiges of the maker of your world.
They are but esoteric rock. Rock which sits.
This isn't making things much clearer to be honest.
Remember the words, the ones written upon the soft tissue at the back of your head.
To tame it - in hubris and pride - will always result in tragedy.
You are a genius at work.
This investigation has led one step closer to that illustrious title you seek.
This magic must be wielded in attunement with the divine! Nothing else.
Listen to the Voice of God. It shan't lead you astray.
That's not the voice you heard, buddy.
That was, if anything, a potential Patron.
Which is, of course, way more exciting.
Hm. Okay. So I'm sensing some different paths here.
You do not know where they shall lead. But you may consider each.
Tell me more about Voice of God.
I'm ready to make a decision. (Choose a feat.)
Urth watches upon you. His spirit channels your powers each day, and that will never change.

Voice of God - Whenever you roll a d20, another hidden d20 is rolled. If that die is the same as your die, you critically succeed. If both are natural twenties, you regain all your hit points, you regain all your spent spell slots, you lose all levels of exhaustion, and you become genuinely happy for 1d4 seconds.
Focus on your mastery of the esoteric.
This knowledge, as cryptic as it is, will help you on your way.

You hold the curled-up Zweihânder in your hands.
Rolled up like a damn scroll!
The curled blade rests in your hands.
ROLL15 wis-Try to understand why it keeps curling up like this.
Maybe I should just leave it. For now.
E-I'll come back to this later.
Try again, later. With this knowledge.
But something tells you this sword is worth the effort.
With all the baggage that comes with such a state.
It appears this blade has a complex.
Specifically, you believe this blade has been used for horrible things in the past.
Thus, the one thing you must do...
Is allow yourself to be tested.
Then, slowly, the blade unfurls. Just a bit. Then...
It's speaking. The sword is actually speaking.
Your goblin companion nods along as the sword begins to speak.
The blade hums with ancient power.
The blade begins to curl again.
Don't start talking like this thing. It's embarrassing.
The blade hums with new purpose.
The sword transforms, in spirit only.
The voice settles into the blade.

The hooded woman's cape flutters as an icy wind rushes through the Tolstad rise.
Her eyes are locked with yours.
She is challenging you, and your manhood. Or she is flirting.
This woman is not trying to intimidate you.
She is probably just not very good at dealing with people.
Her voice is emotionless, and spoken from deep in her chest.
She's being polite. She will not stop.
She stops herself and looks away.
Somehow, this hurts your <u>ego</u> to an intense degree.
She can't just do that! That's... rude!
She does not like you that way. That's more than okay.
Even if it is a bit painful.
She speaks so mechanically. Strange woman.
She knows well how foolish it is.
She looks up to the sky.
This woman has clearly never seen true strength in her life.
It'll swallow Divinii Torum soon enough too. Of course it will.
The Green Dream can live, despite this poor fascist's harsh words.

The Tendrils of The Mender's Court reach far.
You can learn something from this.
Did you see the shit-eating confidence on that elf's luscious lips?
That was the face of order.
A foreign order, fully in control.
...Up until you ripped the shoes off his feet, of course.
I didn't believe a word he was saying.
You could even be forgiven for assaulting that beautiful boy-man. All for those boots.
And yet he said them. To your face. With complete conviction!
Despite the fact that he was clearly lying to your face the entire time.
It was a sweetly covered lie. That's clear in hindsight.
But why else would an Illym Warden (fancy word for ambassador) stand there in the middle of the night, speaking gibberish with some inane cleric?
He had a damn good reason to show up.
And considering they took that corpse right afterwards?
They know exactly what you're doing. It was a highly calculated move to tell you those things.
To blame the dead elf? To clear themselves from any wrongdoing? To sneak away in the night with the biggest piece of evidence?
Before anyone with real authority could get their claws on it?
And most importantly: before the press could get in on the scoop.
You need to learn something from this.
Get yourself closer to that twinky elf's level.
Yes. This is very true. I need to learn from the Illym way.
Ladies don't like style. They like substance.
Yeah. Like... feminist ideologs. Like myself.
Illusions of grandeur can take a man far.
People tend to get swept up alongside it, like little winds trailing in the wake of the greater tempest.
Alas, words are wind. They too fade as the storm turns to gale.
Do not build your empire on words alone. Illym did not.
You remember the Tale of The Mender. It was told to every child.
There have also been three adaptations running on Stage Street over the years. It's one of the classics.
There is some historical preamble. You have more or less memorized it.
Go ahead. (Recall the history of Illym.)
Just tell me the tale. (Skip to the good part.)
I don't care about children's tales. Especially those including elves. (Don't listen to the tale.)
Okay, technically it is. But sure.
The Illym Empire has always been an ominous presence in Askanii.
She was one of the early walking gods. Born as an early tidings of the Divine Era.
She reigned in isolation a very long time.
During the Arcane Era, she used her realm as a wall. One of the few powers which resisted and repelled any Diminorian influence.
Askanii remained wild for many reasons. One of them was the ever-present and self-sustaining elven empire, in their living forest.
Their stasis changed during the Coast War. First by growing tensions on the borders.
They ended up so occupied defending their lands in the east, they did not even intervene when a band of human refugees settled in the troll-infested ruins on the Coast.
Yeah, yeah. They usually have a player chanting those details out before the show starts.
Now, this is when the tale begins...
I've heard this a million times. Can I think about anything else? (Skip it.)
Recall'st thou, the tale of ILLYM THE MENDER?
A great shaman of the wilds, who in divine ascension did bind Askanii's elven kind.
To Norvik she came, emerged upon its streets, scarce years past the Conquest's end-
In shadow wrought, she spells upon each warrior of the Palace, drawing them to URTH in ranks of iron-
Their eyes, bewitched and bright with zeal, beheld her form- a twisting mass of bark and gnarled root-
There URTH did meet her on the steps- a Primal force pressed against the bulwark. Seconds for war. Minutes from passion. Words they shared. Looks exchanged.
The elf-god asked but one question, as she broke her spell upon the great soldiers of the city:
Is anything permitted in a good man's quest?
Thus ends the tale of ILLYM THE MENDER.
For URTH did stay her wrath for mortal men. In years since passed, that elven god hath faded, yet her question lingers still within the Palace air, the streets, the Coast.
If thou know the answer, hold thy tongue and speak it not.
For that beguiling nymph may yet keep watch. Her roots do linger still...
But yeah. This is only the abridged kid's version of course.
It's the one that stuck with you.
The dead elf. This Pinja. She played them all.
Then this not-dead elf, the Warden. He's clearly a professional bullshitter.
They're both master manipulators in their own right. Just excellent, excellent work.
Build up a Beautiful Lie on that (probably) pretty face of yours.
Then you can take over the-
A long line of Empire. An Imperial Dream.
Learn from their mistakes. Build upon their methods. Then you may-
You do realize they're immortal right?
Or they're supposed to be. That's what sets them apart.
Keep that in mind. These bastards are way, way too slow to adapt to anything.
Whether it's a millennial sphinx or an antesepalian celestial or just an elf...
They've all got that weakness. A Fragile Immortality.
If you die, you lose, what? A couple of decades at worst?
I'm ready to make a decision. (Choose a feat.)
An aversion to risk. That's what they've got, times infinity.
So that's what you should learn from them.
Think about that weakness. Use it to your advantage. Keep building that advantage, over and over again.

Imperial Dream - While speaking with someone with less intelligence than yourself, gain an extra proficiency on all d20 rolls.
They'll love you until the day you die.
Beautiful Lie - While you are at full health and 0 exhaustion, gain advantage on all Charisma d20 rolls.
You might even end up actually immortal if you keep this up.

A large merman sits by the sea.
His voice is deep and bubbling.
He is shocked. He does not show it. But he is.
Your presence here is shocking to him.
HE KNOWS YOU AS A DEAD MAN.
He opens his mouth and takes a deep breath.
He does not take his dark blue eyes off the city-horizon.
The goblin is not comfortable around the merman.
Is that fish scale on his skin? Is it real? Is he real?
He swats your hands away like a fly.
He grumbles, and turns to face you.
He's correct. Don't be too aggressive.
He can't tell you off like that!
The merman looks at you with a pair of deep eyes.
The goblin gives you a look.
It says, 'We ain't got time. But maybe it could be useful.'
Oh yeah. He thinks this could be worth it.
Hustling is all about networking, right?
The merman turns to you, slowly.
Snell jumps back and pretends to not be in any way close to you or this conversation.
He looks behind him, glaring at the pile of dirt you left in your wake.
Then the merman closes his intense eyes, letting out a deep (and not-surprisingly salty-smelling) sigh.
He turns back to his rod.
He grumbles, and keeps on fishing.
This means there was a resurrection.
Now you need to learn why.
You were risen for a purpose.
Wow. You must have gotten stuck in something really wacky here.
Be careful now. Someone wanted you dead, and someone wanted you alive. That's bad.
The merman focuses on his fishing.
He does not respond to you.
He grumbles and slowly drops his intense eye contact to instead stare just as intently on the tip of his fishing pole.
It would be quite the tale.
Snell gives you a reassuring nod.
Something akin to, don't worry, you don't need to tell him.
He looks at the paper as you hold it out. Then he slowly grabs it and crumples it in front of you.
Your companion gives you a shrug.
He nods to the second pole.
You keep your eyes peeled on the tip.
He takes a real long breath.
He raises a thick eyebrow in your direction. \
Oh! Is this guy actually rich?
ROLL16 con-Try to not mention it.
He takes a deep and heavy breath.
The merman clenches his jaw- his facial muscles twitching.
Tyrant. It was a good comment.
Yeah, fair. He would have hated it.
The comment... it will slip out. No matter what you do.
Blood fills your mouth. Dripping down the corner of your mouth.
The merman glares at it, as it pools before your boots.
A concerning amount of blood flows from your mouth to the ground.
The merman appears to ignore it.
A big gulp is hard to do, but it happens.
The metallic taste fills your entire being.
Snell says nothing, but gives you a shocked, and quite honestly sad, expression.
For once, wearing a helmet is actually helpful.
Now ask him something. He'll indulge you.
Man to man. Hu-man to mer-man.
It is very, very beautiful, this thing. Make sure to ask interesting questions about him. That will make him understand how much you enjoy this special time with him.
.TS_CatchFish==0-(Wait an hour to see if you catch something.)
He realized he spoke a bit too much.
Thalassos does not want to discuss politics.
Hard at work! This is a real entrepreneur.
He leans in towards you with a stern look on his face.
Just as you're about to give up, you see something tug at the tip of the pole.
Thalassos sighs and keeps looking at his own pole.
He reaches forward and grabs the second pole.
Grabbing it from you, while continuing to fish on his own.
With a stern nod, he sends you on your way.
The merman stays silent for a while. Then he mutters to you.
The merman says nothing for a while.
He does not wish to debate you.
He is not the political kind.
He actually turns to face you with a disgusted expression.
Then he turns back, and keeps on fishing.
The merman does not respond or react.
The merman says nothing for a few seconds, as if feigning a think.
Like a father handling his slightly too-old-for-these-types-of-questions son.
HE CATCHES YOU TAKING HIS STUFF.

You pick up the newspaper. It feels flimsy and light, like a cheap magazine.
But the lettering is printed in a glistening and wonderfully thematic GREEN.
A very important choice. A GOOD choice.
But the lettering is printed in a deep crimson red.
And the lettering is printed in a barely readable yellow font.
Nice. That'll make it harder for idiots to get taken by the silly Azgalist op-eds.
And the lettering is printed in a faded black ink.
(Read the editorial, WE BUILT THIS CITY.)
Most of the paper is about the election- as it lists out and goes over all the main Azgalian talking points.
Working with the previous government to transition into an Azgalian Social Democratic state.
And, in some minor ways, support for the Clanlands.
You open the page, your hands GRIPPING the paper.
WE BUILT THIS CITY - AND THEY STOLE IT FROM US

The cobblestones beneath our feet tell a story. Every cobblestone was laid by working hands. Every grand building rose from the sweat of laborers. Every bridge was built by those who may never own the homes they can see across the water. We built this city- and now we're told to be grateful for scraps on our tables.
<i>The Freestriders speak fair and feel foul. They are not coming- they are already here. They will turn Norvik into another corpse of a \
The time for compromise is over. We see what they've done elsewhere. Prosperity for the few. Poverty for the many. Our wealth - created by OUR hands - shipped east to line foreign pockets.
Full rights for cross-folk relationships and gender-affirmed individuals
Every strike, every protest, every act of resistance moves us closer to the city we deserve. The ruling class fears us because they know the truth: there are more of us than them.

Don't wait for permission. Don't wait for the next election. Don't wait for moderate politicians to find their courage. The workers of Norvik have built everything of value in this city. Now we must CLAIM what is ours.
We built this city. We run this city. We ARE this city.
Her heart cries out for change.
Eh. It's a bit too radical to go for the casual crowd.
Reads more like Radaz meeting notes.

The cobblestones beneath our feet tell a story. Every brick in every building, every bridge spanning our canals- they speak of generations of Norvikian toil. As we approach the elections, we must remember whose sweat and sacrifice created the prosperity we see today.

Recent events have shown us the challenges our city faces. The protests at the Towers, ongoing disputes with various guilds, and concerns about the Golden Line policies have highlighted the gap between those who have ruled Norvik after Urth's fading, and Norvik's working families.

We cannot ignore legitimate concerns about economic inequality and the concentration of wealth. When guild membership fees burden working families, when our prosperity flows eastward instead of benefiting those who created it, we must act - but we must act wisely.
<i>The Freestriders want to turn Norvik into another \
Advocate for policies that put Norvik's wealth in the hands of its people
Reform guild membership structures to ensure fair access
Support working families through expanded social programs
Continue building bridges between all communities in our diverse city

Change takes time. Real change, lasting change, comes through democratic processes and building coalitions. The people of Norvik have power - the power of organization, the power of the vote, the power of solidarity.
Use that power wisely. Support candidates who understand that progress means more than campaign promises.
We built this city. We'll decide its future.
A calm yet well-written ask for unity and progressive ideas.

Working with the previous government to transition into an Azgalian Social Democratic state.
And, in some minor ways, support for the Clanlands.
You open the page, your hands GRIPPING the paper.
WE BUILT THIS CITY - AND THEY STOLE IT FROM US

The cobblestones beneath our feet tell a story. Every cobblestone was laid by working hands. Every grand building rose from the sweat of laborers. Every bridge was built by those who may never own the homes they can see across the water. We built this city- and now we're told to be grateful for scraps on our tables.
<i>The Freestriders speak fair and feel foul. They are not coming- they are already here. They will turn Norvik into another corpse of a \
The time for compromise is over. We see what they've done elsewhere. Prosperity for the few. Poverty for the many. Our wealth - created by OUR hands - shipped east to line foreign pockets.
Full rights for cross-folk relationships and gender-affirmed individuals
Every strike, every protest, every act of resistance moves us closer to the city we deserve. The ruling class fears us because they know the truth: there are more of us than them.

Don't wait for permission. Don't wait for the next election. Don't wait for moderate politicians to find their courage. The workers of Norvik have built everything of value in this city. Now we must CLAIM what is ours.
We built this city. We run this city. We ARE this city.
Her heart cries out for change.
Eh. It's a bit too radical to go for the casual crowd.
Reads more like Radaz meeting notes.

The cobblestones beneath our feet tell a story. Every brick in every building, every bridge spanning our canals- they speak of generations of Norvikian toil. As we approach the elections, we must remember whose sweat and sacrifice created the prosperity we see today.

Recent events have shown us the challenges our city faces. The protests at the Towers, ongoing disputes with various guilds, and concerns about the Golden Line policies have highlighted the gap between those who have ruled Norvik after Urth's fading, and Norvik's working families.

We cannot ignore legitimate concerns about economic inequality and the concentration of wealth. When guild membership fees burden working families, when our prosperity flows eastward instead of benefiting those who created it, we must act - but we must act wisely.
<i>The Freestriders want to turn Norvik into another \
Advocate for policies that put Norvik's wealth in the hands of its people
Reform guild membership structures to ensure fair access
Support working families through expanded social programs
Continue building bridges between all communities in our diverse city

Change takes time. Real change, lasting change, comes through democratic processes and building coalitions. The people of Norvik have power - the power of organization, the power of the vote, the power of solidarity.
Use that power wisely. Support candidates who understand that progress means more than campaign promises.
We built this city. We'll decide its future.
A calm yet well-written ask for unity and progressive ideas.
You see a halfling girl standing by the shrine. She gives you a tired glance.
She groans and rolls her eyes.
The painful dismissal of a teenage girl.
You are annoying her. You should go.
She is waiting for someone and wants you to leave.
However, there is something else- heavy on her heart...
You stand your ground before the halfling.
Her angsty eye rolls hit you hard, but...
They are nothing for your nerves of steel.
Translation: Fuck off. I care a lot.
You try to stand your ground before the halfling.
She glares at you. Her eyes looking you up and down.
She is about to call you the word.
The one word to break you.
The halfling girl opens her mouth to speak.
Your goblin companion just shakes his head.
The halfling girl refuses to talk to you.
She leans over, looking nervously behind you.
Does she even know what that means?
You hand it over. She looks a bit shocked.
The halfling just stares at you.
Oh no. You should leave. NOW.
Oh la la. Late-night rendezvous? How scandalous.
But yeah. You should go. Now.
Her rage almost makes her growl at you.
She stomps her foot into the hard stone.
She crosses her arms and strikes a stoic pose.
A darkness falls upon her face.
The Golden Horde's invasion into western Askanii. It is quite horrific.
Right, there's some type of invasion going on in the west.
A conflict based on many geopolitical goals.
Of course. It's common knowledge that most young halflings are more or less in active resistance against the Golden Horde.
The Commonwealth is a broken nation of a lazy people.
They could not survive even the most basic of Celestial trickery.
The Clanlands are in shambles due to an illegal subjugation and annexation.
They need all the support they can get. It's only right to speak up.
They are good and honorable people.
What does it mean to be strong, if not to protect those faced with injustice?
Eh. They're not exactly the most productive people on the Coast.
And they've always been isolationist. That's probably the main reason they invaded: there's a bunch of expensive Amber sitting underneath the western Clanlands.
If they're not gonna mine it, somebody will.
Just say something nice about halflings. No need to get all preachy.
She gives you a tired look.
Snell gives you a slightly intrigued look.
Meek does not give you a look. You don't think he's even listening.

It's quite light, some kind of magazine paper. Unrolling it reveals a short statement about a political rally at the Circle Square.
'Permission of Public Gathering ACCEPTED' it says in big letters at the bottom, followed by a signature. 'Tolstad Magistrate, Nicholas Darrow.'
The actual text uses an archaic and typically bureaucratic writing style.
Have I seen this kind of document before?
You scan the text again, checking for spelling, phrasing, and terminology.
It is perfect. Not a single error.
The date. It says their rally is supposed to be held on the 16th.
The document is perfectly legal. But it's expired.
It's perfect. Not a single error.
You should check it out again later.
Or ask someone who knows about this kind of stuff.
Then again, you already know the problem here. It is expired. No need to waste time on going over it again. Unless you really want to. Just for the 'experience'.
In your scribing duty, you've signed these a number of times.
Because it's a legal document? Duh?

The scent of smoke fills your helmet as you approach the hooded goblin.
He takes a drag from the pipe, his rough face illuminated.
He is smiling at you, beneath the mask. He is your friend.
The hooded goblin does not reply.
The hooded goblin does not reply at first.
Then he just looks to Snell with a foul frown.
You hear him clear his throat, slowly.
As your foot is about to hit him in the shin, the goblin reveals a dagger from his cloak.
Faster than you could spot, he's got it pointed towards your boot.
You manage to stop yourself just in time. How fast is this guy?
You barely manage to stop yourself from stabbing yourself on his dagger. How fast is this guy?
He then slowly retracts his dagger, hiding it as smoothly as he pulled it out.
The hooded goblin glances at you as you approach.
He appears to not enjoy your company.
The hooded goblin lets out a cloud of smoke.
Pure rage is building in your companion.
He only scoffs at Snell's question.
Both of them just quietly glare at each other.
You didn't even need to pick up on anything there.
That is some very vague intent. Even by your standards.
You grab a half-filled glass from the table as the goblin watches your movements closely.
What a powerful and manly move.
You should put it down before he gets mad.
Your companion gives you a careful look.
He wonders what the hell you're about to do.
Time to decide what 'glass him' means, eh?
Guess you can make it mean anything you want.
ROLL16 str-(Smash it into his face.)
.DS_RazzPoured==0-(Pour out the alcoholic content onto the floor.)
You violently crush the glass into the face of the goblin.
He groans as the glassy shards stick into his rough skin.
The hooded goblin sits there, glaring at you for a second. Blood seeping out of his face.
He barely even reacts to having glass shoved into his face.
Both your horrible action and his reaction.
The goblin reaches to his side.
He doesn't take a weapon. He instead opens a pouch.
Grabs some leaves inside, crushes them in his hands - all while his bleeding eyes remain fixated on you - and then snorts the powder up his nose.
You cannot see, but this must be some type of Primal magic.
Within seconds the glass shards begin to pop out of his face, trickling to the ground.
The bleeding stops, any running blood evaporating from his face.
He dusts off the final shards of glass from his cloak.
As you violently attempt to press the glass into the goblin's face, he reacts.
Swinging his cloak around, blocking your vision from its target.
Before you realize, the goblin has grabbed his glass back, and moved underneath your legs in one fell swoop.
A blade pressed firmly against your inner thigh.
The hooded goblin slowly removes his blade and takes a sip from the glass.
The hooded goblin slowly removes his blade and places the empty glass on the table again.
Then he places the glass back onto the table.
Not toasting back? He surely deserves a glassing.
He is unable to actually attack you.
He follows your movements as you slowly put it back where you took it.
He picks up his pipe from the ground, taking a big drag.
Geez. The fact he's just sitting there and not killing you outright is... interesting.
This goblin, for some reason, is unable to harm you.
He leans back in the seat, blowing smoke in the air.
He lets out a smoky scoff.
Those who get trampled among the Strings.
You are surely the first type.
He gives the hooded goblin a quick bow. \
Then he gives you a thumbs up. \
Underneath his hood, you see a smirk spread on the maw of the goblin.
Your companion turns to you and mutters, \
He blows out a big cloud of foul-smelling smoke.
It's some kind of Vourgeni-bush in his pipe. Awful.
As your hand is just about to reach into his coat pocket, you suddenly feel cold steel pressed up against your chin, underneath your helmet.
It pierces your skin, immediately sending droplets of blood to fall down on the hooded goblin's hand.
(Lean in harder. Prove that you're immune to piercing damage.)
The pain causes your muscles to shake.
You see his eyes widen. Yet he doesn't move the dagger one inch.
The blood now pours down the blade and his entire arm.
Lean in hard. Don't give in.
You can feel the dagger deep in your flesh.
In an instant the hooded goblin retracts his bloodied dagger out of your head.
He quickly wipes off the worst of the blood.
You've earned his respect. An insane type of respect, but respect nonetheless.
This guy is a crazed killer, and you've just intimidated him.
Your strength of will is commendable.
Cease this behavior. You will not last long otherwise.
The dagger is removed from your chin, yet the pain remains.
He retracts his dagger, wiping off the worst of the blood.
Yet on his lips: a shocked smile.
He can't help but grin like a fool.

The thing in the fire makes a few scared noises, and then pretends to be nothing but a strange elemental mask on the pyre.
You should walk over to the other side of the table, if you want to speak with the celestial.
You look into the flames of the fireplace.
A hissing voice calls out to you.
Your eyes adjust to the brightness of the fire.
A figure lies upon the lit timber.
Like a puff of smoke, materialized into a solid chunk.
It is a little smoke guy. They appear at times.
This one appears to be quite heat-related.
It is a Mephit. A primal spirit.
This one appears to be made out of smoke and heat.
The creature lets out a groan and rolls around on the fireplace.
It looks back and forth between you and Snell.
Your goblin companion squints into the fire.
Oh, really? Test the little guy.
They must have some kind of secret around here.
.AR_HiddenSafe==0-(Grab a piece of wood splinter off the ground.) \
The little guy gives you a smoky expression.
You have a feeling the mephit could give something away.
It leans back in the flames and mutters in a language you don't understand.
It means something like 'Fucking hells, this is very tiring to me.'.
It leans out of the fireplace and gestures towards the mirror in the corner.
(Look at the mirror, and then back to the mephit.)
He rolls around a bit on the flames, swearing to himself.
It snaps its little claw-like fingers, then turns back to you while laughing.
It sighs. A puff of smoke wisps into the air.
ROLL14 con-Prove that you're a worthy elemental lord by sticking your hand in the fire.
The little creature stashes away the coins in a corner of the fireplace.
Money can warp all laws of reality and esoteric forces.
What would he need money for?
This elemental does not seek value out of your coinage.
He most likely enjoys the material itself.
It stares back for a moment.
Then it blows a puff of ashy smoke in your face.
(Place your hand in front of the smoke guy, right in the flames.)
The intense heat wraps around your flesh.
With your second hand, you cast druidcraft, causing the flame to wrap around your hand.
It is not a lot- but you can sway the flames by tugging Primal Strings.
(Slowly remove your damaged hand from the fire.)
Your heartbeat screams in your ears.
Beyond your body, the smoke creature laughs wildly.
You hear him cackling as he rolls over and stares into the wall.
With a shaky hand, you reach forth.
The intense pain of searing flames makes it impossible to hold it in the fire.
You see the smoke creature cackling wildly, rolling around on the timber.
The creature cackles in the fireplace.
Look, you gotta be a bit more cool about this.
At least pretend to be good at stealing.
The mephit flares up from the fireplace.
From the whirl of smoke and ash- it sends a blast of heat towards you.

A dozen dwarves and an orc walk into a tavern.
The orc turns to the tavernkeep and says, 'hey, give me a large one'.
Please don't turn this into a joke.
These are real people you're talking about.
Let me continue. So... the dwarves go-
The dwarves have struggled as a minority for decades. They deserve respect, nothing less.
The political struggle is never a joke.
Words are sharp. Carelessly aimed, they can easily cut the chord of any man.
A true statement. To lighten the mood is to ignore the very real pain in our realm.
You're divided. But a truth will be achieved.
Whatever. Look, the gist of the story is: the orc is hard at work, improving this shitty little city. The dwarves are annoying and stupid.
Get outta here! Ain't your scene, testy.
You believe for a second the orc wants this city to improve?
No. You've seen too much to ever believe such a lie.
This is some kind of debate, eh?
The orc is with the Freestriders, right? So she's with the good guys.
The dwarves are Azgalists. That means they like poor people, thus making them the good guys.
They're a bunch of lazy assholes!
All they wanna do is take from the ones who work and give it to those who don't.
The Revolutionary Freestrider will bring freedom, democracy, and riches to Norvik.
Last part being the most poignant.
Let us focus on each of those points. One at a time.
Freedom. Freestrider rule would entail joining the Free City Alliance.
Local politics will be focused entirely upon serving the core cities in Reeds, and the interest of the Trade Company.
Freedom. Freestrider would only focus on helping the rich. And so on.

Democracy. While it is true that they initiated the move towards universal suffrage, the Freestriders are known for both ruthlessly pushing propaganda to vulnerable folk, as well as just simply buying votes.
Democracy. They do like that. But they're really corrupt also.
Riches. The claim that Freestriderism would somehow enrich the folk of Norvik is actually ridiculous.
What kind of riches are being distributed to the lower class of the city, with the already Freestrider-owned factories?
Riches. They might bring a lot of money into the city, but it's always going to the factory owners and already rich folks.
This is silly. Without Freestriders you'd probably be a farmer. Who the fuck wants to be a farmer?
Do you two never shut up?
I feel like I should make a decision here. (Choose feat.)
Yes. You must retain the spirit of the Azgalian revolution.
After completing a Quest Branch, you can allow it to Take Root in your mind. When doing this, you must make a choice between available feats.
Take a deep breath, and stand up against the ever-growing wave of capitalism.
With this feat, you restore 1 hit point for every hour that passes.
Greed ain't so bad! To be able to unlock a better life for yourself? Who wouldn't want that?
With this feat, you gain 50% more Crowns on pickup.
Now, keep an eye out for any opportunity. There are crowns to be made in every corner of this stinking place.
You've chosen well. True harmony comes from being in-tune with your fellow folk.
The dwarven way is the GOOD way. The egalitarian way.
(Feel like you've resolved some conflict in the world.)
